fwSched.summary: himmy ships a near-complete, production-hardened "Routines" subsystem: a routine is a saved prompt bound to an agent that fires unattended on a schedule, runs through the exact same pipeline as an interactive run (lands in the runs store, obeys HITL/approval gating, can deliver its result via Telegram/email), and is durable across restarts. Three recent commits built it out: Phase 0 (559cb0d) added a standalone `himmy worker` process so routines fire with NO API server plus an OS-cron bridge (`himmy routines run-now`); Phase 1 (cef0823) added cron expressions, per-routine IANA timezones, and missed-run/catch-up policies; the multi-node commit (63ea053) added sleep-to-next-fire precision, Postgres leader election, and per-tenant fairness/quotas. The whole layer is reachable as a library: the store (`RoutinesStore` / `get_routines_store()`), the scheduler (`RoutineScheduler` / `get_scheduler()`), pure schedule math (`himmy.api.routine_schedule`), the shared startup bootstrap (`himmy.api.runtime_bootstrap`), a CLI (`himmy routines ...`, `himmy worker`), and two HTTP routers. The key architectural fact for scholar-desk: routines are stored in SQLite at `.himmy/routines.db` (cwd-keyed) and fire ONLY when something runs the scheduler — either the full himmy server, OR `himmy worker`, OR by directly calling `RoutineScheduler.start()` from scholar-desk's own FastAPI lifespan. scholar-desk currently runs its own custom FastAPI app (`src/himmy_app/server.py`, NOT himmy's BFF) and registers local tools (e.g. `src/himmy_app/connectors/google_calendar.py`), so it must opt into scheduling explicitly — nothing fires today. Maturity is overwhelmingly "built" and e2e-verified on real models + live Postgres; the only soft spots are that the single-user-local path is SQLite-only (cron is an optional `himmy[cron]` extra via cronsim) and deliver kinds are limited to none/telegram/email.

capabilities.name: Routines store + Routine/Schedule models (the durable definition layer)
capabilities.whatItDoes: Defines and persists a 'routine' — a saved prompt bound to an agent (by filesystem agent_path for single-user-local, or by stored agent_id for multi-tenant) that runs on a schedule. The Schedule model supports four kinds: daily (HH:MM), every (N hours, 1..168), cron (5-field expr), and at (one-shot ISO instant), plus timezone, missed-run policy (coalesce/skip/run_each), max_catchup, grace_seconds, and overlap. Persisted to SQLite at .himmy/routines.db with an additive migration; tracks last_run_at/last_status/last_preview/last_error and next_fire_at.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.api.routines.Routine(name=, agent_path=, prompt=, schedule=Schedule(...), provider=, model=, deliver=, enabled=); himmy.api.routines.Schedule(kind='daily'|'every'|'cron'|'at', at=, hours=, expr=, at_datetime=, timezone=, missed=, max_catchup=, grace_seconds=); store = himmy.api.routines.get_routines_store(); store.upsert(routine) -> Routine; store.list(workspace_id=...) ; store.get(id, workspace_id=...); store.delete(id, workspace_id=...); store.count(workspace_id=, enabled_only=); Schedule.describe(). DB path overridable via HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH.
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk imports himmy.api.routines directly (it already imports himmy.api.* e.g. studio_google). Because get_routines_store() resolves .himmy/routines.db relative to cwd, scholar-desk's server should run from a stable working dir (or set HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH to its data dir, alongside the existing .scholar-desk data dir convention) so routines survive restarts. Routines created here default to the __local__ workspace and bind by agent_path to a project agent.yaml — exactly the single-user-local seam a desktop app wants. scholar-desk would expose CRUD over its OWN FastAPI app by calling these store methods (it does not need himmy's /v1 router).
capabilities.appOpportunity: Lets the user save reusable automations in the workspace app: 'every morning at 7:00, summarize my new arXiv papers', 'every 6h, check for new citations', a one-shot 'at 2026-07-01T09:00 remind me to renew'. The UI is just a list of saved routines with schedule + last-run status, backed entirely by this store.

capabilities.name: RoutineScheduler — the in-process firing loop (sleep-to-next-fire)
capabilities.whatItDoes: The asyncio loop that ticks, finds due routines (is_due math per kind), and launches them through the unattended pipeline. It is sleep-to-next-fire: it computes the soonest boundary across all enabled routines (next_fire) and sleeps until then, capped by HIMMY_SCHEDULER_MAX_SLEEP (default 30s), and wakes instantly on CRUD via notify_change(). One bad tick never kills the loop; no overlapping execution of the same routine; per-run wall-clock timeout (HIMMY_ROUTINE_TIMEOUT_S, default 300s). On launch it can apply each routine's missed-run/catch-up policy.
capabilities.publicApi: sched = himmy.api.routines.get_scheduler(); sched.start() (idempotent); await sched.stop(); sched.notify_change() (wake after CRUD); sched.is_running(routine_id) -> bool; await sched.run_now(routine_id) -> Routine (raises RoutineBusyError on overlap); await sched.catch_up_on_launch() -> dict[id, action]; sched.tick() -> list[str]. Enable/disable globally via HIMMY_ROUTINES_SCHEDULER env (on/off).
capabilities.integrationNotes: This is the cheapest integration for scholar-desk: in its FastAPI startup (server.py create_app), call himmy.api.routines.get_scheduler().start() and await get_scheduler().catch_up_on_launch(); on shutdown await get_scheduler().stop(). After any CRUD from scholar-desk's routes call get_scheduler().notify_change() so a new/edited routine re-plans immediately (this is exactly what himmy's studio_routines router does via its _wake_scheduler helper). No extra process needed for a single-user desktop app. The scheduler runs every tenant's routines, but in __local__-only scholar-desk that's just the user's own.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Makes the saved automations actually fire while the app/back-end is running: scheduled daily news digest, recurring library enrichment, morning briefing — all run on the same agent brain scholar-desk already uses, with results landing where interactive runs do.

capabilities.name: Standalone `himmy worker` — fire routines + drain the run queue with no API server (Phase 0)
capabilities.whatItDoes: A single CLI process that brings up the SAME durable substrate the FastAPI server's lifespan builds (durable container, dispatch enabled, RunDispatcher for the durable run-queue, plus the RoutineScheduler) and blocks until SIGINT/SIGTERM, then shuts down gracefully. Closes the gap where routines silently never fire outside the full server. Modes: default (scheduler + queue worker), --no-scheduler (queue only), --scheduler-only (tick only). Requires a durable run store (auto-sets HIMMY_DURABLE_STORAGE=1; Postgres via HIMMY_DATABASE_URL). --store sets the SQLite path, --concurrency sets dispatch concurrency.
capabilities.publicApi: CLI: `himmy worker [--store PATH] [--concurrency N] [--no-scheduler|--scheduler-only]`. Programmatic equivalent: himmy.cli.commands.cmd_worker(args) / _run_worker(run_scheduler=, run_dispatcher=); or build it yourself from himmy.api.runtime_bootstrap.build_durable_container() + start_run_substrate() + stop_run_substrate().
capabilities.integrationNotes: Two options for scholar-desk. (A) If it prefers a SEPARATE background process (so scheduling survives even when the UI server is restarted), the Electron main process can spawn `himmy worker --store <data-dir>/runs.db` on app launch and kill it on quit. (B) If it wants everything in one process, skip the worker and just start RoutineScheduler in its own FastAPI lifespan (see RoutineScheduler row) — the dispatcher/durable-queue is only needed for the multi-tenant agent_id path, which scholar-desk doesn't use. The runbook docs/enterprise/worker-and-cron-bridge.md is the reference.
capabilities.appOpportunity: An always-on background automations engine for the Electron app: even with the front-end closed, the spawned worker keeps firing the morning briefing / news digest / library enrichment, so they're ready when the user opens the workspace.

capabilities.name: OS-cron bridge — `himmy routines run-now` (durable single fire)
capabilities.whatItDoes: Runs one routine immediately through the full unattended rails and records a durable, lineage-tracked run (RUN_* spine events + actor.routine_id) in the same canonical store the server/`himmy runs`/Studio read. A cross-process flock refuses a concurrent second run of the same routine (CLI run-now and the scheduler can never double-execute). Designed as the bridge for OS cron / systemd timers / launchd / Electron schedulers that want to own the clock instead of the in-process loop.
capabilities.publicApi: CLI: `himmy routines run-now <id> [--json]`. Programmatic: await himmy.api.routines.execute_routine(routine_id) (single-user agent_path path) — flock-guarded, raises RoutineBusyError on contention; returns the refreshed Routine with last_status. get_scheduler().run_now(id) for the in-process awaited variant.
capabilities.integrationNotes: If scholar-desk (or its Electron shell) would rather drive timing with the OS scheduler (launchd on macOS) than keep a Python loop alive, register a launchd job that runs `himmy routines run-now <id>` (or call himmy.api.routines.execute_routine(id) from a tiny script). This gives durable single fires without keeping a long-lived scheduler process. The flock guarantees it won't collide with an in-process scheduler if both exist.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Lets the macOS app schedule a 'wake the machine and run my 7am briefing' via launchd even when nothing else is running, and gives a manual 'Run now' button on each routine in the UI that goes through the identical durable path as the scheduled fire.

capabilities.name: Cron expressions + per-routine IANA timezones + catch-up (Phase 1 schedule math)
capabilities.whatItDoes: Pure, side-effect-free timezone-aware scheduling math, isolated for golden-testing. cron_next_fire computes the next 5-field-cron occurrence IN the routine's IANA timezone then normalises to UTC (DST-correct; verified for the non-integer +5:45 Asia/Kathmandu offset; uses cronsim, which fires a DST fall-back exactly once — croniter was rejected for double-firing). Timezone resolves explicit -> env HIMMY_TZ -> host-local. Missed-run/catch-up reads the authoritative last_run_at anchor (clock-skew safe): coalesce (fire once now), skip (drop the gap), run_each (backfill each missed occurrence, hard-capped by max_catchup and grace_seconds so a long laptop sleep can't enqueue an unbounded backlog).
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.api.routine_schedule.cron_next_fire(expr, after_utc, zone); cron_occurrences_between(expr, after, until, zone, limit=); resolve_zone(tz_name|None) -> ZoneInfo; default_timezone_name(); validate_cron(expr); validate_timezone(name); cron_is_available() -> bool. Surfaced through Schedule(kind='cron', expr=, timezone=) and CLI flags --cron/--timezone/--missed-run/--overlap. Optional dep: `pip install himmy[cron]` (cronsim).
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk should add the [cron] extra to its himmy install (its requirements.txt currently pins himmy[toolkit,api,openai,embeddings]; add cron) IF it wants cron-grammar routines — otherwise daily/every work with zero extra deps and cron strings are accepted at create-time but error only at fire. For a Nepal-based user, set HIMMY_TZ=Asia/Kathmandu (or pass timezone per routine) so 'daily 07:00' means 07:00 local. The catch-up policy is the laptop-sleep story that matters for a desktop app: coalesce (the default) means a routine missed while the Mac was asleep fires exactly once on wake.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Precise, human-friendly schedules in the workspace app: 'weekdays at 6:30 Kathmandu time' (cron 30 6 * * 1-5), one-shot reminders, and laptop-sleep-safe behavior so the morning briefing still appears once after the lid was closed overnight instead of silently skipping or firing a storm of backfills.

capabilities.name: Result delivery + lifecycle notifications (Telegram / email / in-app)
capabilities.whatItDoes: When a routine finishes, _deliver sends the result text through a configured himmy connection (deliver='telegram' or 'email'; capped at 4000 chars; failures are recorded, never raised) and _notify records an in-app notification ('Routine ran', 'Routine needs approval', 'Routine failed') with a deep link. Approval-gated tools PAUSE the run as awaiting_approval and surface a notification pointing at the Approvals inbox — the scheduler never auto-approves.
capabilities.publicApi: Set Routine.deliver = 'none'|'telegram'|'email'. Delivery uses himmy.api.studio_connections.get_connection/send_via_connection; notifications use himmy.api.routers.studio_notify.record_notification. DeliverKind and DELIVERY_MAX_CHARS exported from himmy.api.routines.
capabilities.integrationNotes: Telegram/email delivery requires the corresponding himmy connection to be configured (studio_connections). scholar-desk could reuse the email path (SMTP) for a self-emailed digest with minimal wiring, or rely on the in-app notification record and render it in its own UI. To add a custom delivery channel (e.g. a desktop notification or writing into scholar-desk's own notes/inbox), scholar-desk would post-process the routine result itself rather than relying on the built-in deliver kinds, since only none/telegram/email exist.
capabilities.appOpportunity: The morning briefing / news digest lands somewhere the user sees it: an email to self, a Telegram ping, or an in-app notification badge — and an automation that hits a tool needing approval safely parks itself in an Approvals queue instead of acting unattended.

capabilities.name: Multi-node leader election + per-tenant fairness/quotas (production hardening)
capabilities.whatItDoes: Hardens scheduling for multi-node/multi-tenant deployments. scheduler_leader does Postgres leader election via a session pg_try_advisory_lock (only the leader ticks; clean failover when the leader dies; catch-up covers the gap; per-fire dedup-CAS prevents double-fire during overlap). On SQLite it's a same-host flock guard + a loud cross-host warning. The dispatcher adds opt-in per-tenant fairness (least-loaded-first claim) and HARD per-tenant quotas (max enabled routines -> 429, catch-up clamp, outstanding-run cap) fused atomically so one tenant can't starve or flood the shared queue. All additive/opt-in/fail-open; single-tenant behaviour is byte-identical.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.api.scheduler_leader.acquire_scheduler_leadership(container, require_single_scheduler_ack=) -> SchedulerLeadership(is_leader, mode, ...); .refresh()/.release(). Quota knobs: HIMMY_TENANT_MAX_ROUTINES, HIMMY_TENANT_MAX_CATCHUP, HIMMY_DISPATCH_FAIRNESS, HIMMY_SCHEDULER_REQUIRE_ACK, HIMMY_SCHEDULER_SINGLE_NODE_ACK; helpers tenant_max_routines()/tenant_max_catchup()/create_routine_with_quota(). The __local__ workspace is exempt from all quotas.
capabilities.integrationNotes: Mostly irrelevant for a single-user desktop scholar-desk: it runs one node, one __local__ workspace (which is exempt from quotas), and SQLite — so leader election degrades to the same-host flock guard and quotas/fairness are no-ops. scholar-desk gets the SAFETY (the flock stops a stray second scheduler from double-firing if both the in-process loop and a `himmy worker` are accidentally running) for free, without configuring anything. This layer only becomes relevant if scholar-desk ever moves its back-end to a shared Postgres / multi-instance deployment.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Future-proofing: if scholar-desk ever runs as a hosted multi-user service, the same routines layer already supports exactly-once firing across replicas and per-user fairness/quotas with no rewrite — today it simply guarantees a single-user desktop never double-fires a scheduled briefing.

capabilities.name: HTTP routers for routines (REST CRUD + run-now) — optional, for reference
capabilities.whatItDoes: Two FastAPI routers expose routines over HTTP: the multi-tenant /v1/routines control-plane router (POST/GET/PATCH/DELETE + POST /{id}/run-now, RBAC-gated, agent_id-bound, no agent_path to avoid FS leaks, idempotency keys, per-tenant quota 429s) and the single-user studio_routines router (the GUI surface that also owns starting/stopping the scheduler and waking it on CRUD).
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.api.routers.routines.router (prefix /v1/routines) with CreateRoutineRequest/UpdateRoutineRequest/RoutineView/RoutineListResponse; himmy.api.routers.studio_routines.router (Studio). Mountable via app.include_router(...).
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk runs its OWN custom FastAPI app (src/himmy_app/server.py), deliberately NOT himmy's BFF, so it likely should NOT mount these routers wholesale (the /v1 one carries RBAC/multi-tenant machinery scholar-desk doesn't use). Instead, the cleaner pattern matches how scholar-desk already wraps himmy: write a handful of thin endpoints in its own server.py that call get_routines_store() + get_scheduler() directly (mirroring what studio_routines.py does, minus the multi-tenant bits). The studio_routines router is the best copy-from reference for the scheduler start/notify_change/catch_up wiring.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Gives scholar-desk's React front-end a clean way to list/create/edit/delete/run-now routines from the UI — implemented as a few endpoints on its existing FastAPI app that delegate to the himmy store + scheduler, powering a 'Automations' or 'Schedules' panel in the workspace.

fwKernels.area: himmy framework — non-scheduling reusable capabilities for scholar-desk (himmy-as-library)
fwKernels.summary: Mapped the himmy framework (package `himmy` at /Users/samriddhagc/LocalProjects/himmy-agent-test, source under `himmy/`, NOT `src/himmy`) against scholar-desk's current usage. scholar-desk (src/himmy_app) already consumes a meaningful slice: the memory pack (durable remember/recall via register_memory_pack), the knowledge/RAG kernel with hybrid BM25+dense + cross-encoder rerank (papers_rag.py builds a himmy KnowledgeBase directly), the PlannerOrchestrator + reflect() (research.py deep_research), SSE streaming via stream_task (server.py /ask/stream), the SqliteSessionStore + SqliteCheckpointStore, and the tasks/google built-in packs. What it does NOT yet adopt — and where the leverage is for a research/productivity desktop app — is: (1) durable memory's richer layers (bi-temporal recall, the typed memory GRAPH, audited consolidation, and the auto-injection MemoryContextAdapter); (2) the durable on-disk SqliteKnowledgeBackend (so the papers index survives restarts instead of re-embedding every launch — explicitly designed for this Daybook case) plus the genuine local embedders (fastembed/Ollama via build_embedder "auto"); (3) the guardrails/DLP pipeline (PII redaction, prompt-injection, a SQLite-backed reversible token vault) on the input/output/tool surfaces; (4) SELF-LEARNING (opt-in spec flag self_learning, with outcome_weight + trajectory_hints) that mines OUTCOME_SCORED/TOOL_FAILED events into per-tool reputation, reorders flaky tools, injects reliability/loop hints, and feeds back user thumbs via OutcomeRecorder; (5) multi-agent orchestrators (handoff/delegate teams, group-chat, StateGraph) for a multi-step research council; (6) the BFCL-grade eval/benchmark harness for regression-testing the agent over local models; (7) the connector SDK proper (OutboundToolConnector.register_tools — connector-as-tool with SSRF guard, secrets layer, rate limit, idempotency, HITL requires_approval) which scholar-desk re-implements ad hoc; (8) constrained/structured decoding (CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY + ResponseFormat) for guaranteed-JSON extraction; (9) the EntityRecord audit spine + run-event stream for a verifiable activity/provenance log. Everything is offline-first and best-effort/opt-in by design, so each can be wired incrementally without destabilizing the existing app.

capabilities.name: Durable user MEMORY (remember/recall + temporal + graph + consolidation)
capabilities.whatItDoes: MemoryService persists facts per subject_id and recalls them by cosine similarity over an embedder, reading from the store every time so a SqliteMemoryStore survives restarts. Beyond simple recall it is bi-temporal (as_of point-in-time, active_only, invalidate-not-delete), Letta-tiered (core/recall/archival with promote()), relational (typed MemoryLink graph + traverse_graph + recall(max_hops=...) relational expansion), and audited (projects each write onto the EntityRecord spine + emits MEMORY_REMEMBERED/RECALLED events when a registry/sink is wired). MemoryConsolidator reconciles a new fact against existing ones as ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP instead of blindly appending. MemoryContextAdapter auto-injects recalled memories into the system prompt with a noise floor.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.services.memory: MemoryService(store, embedder=, min_similarity=, registry=).remember(text, subject_id=, tier=, stable_key=) / await recall(query, subject_id=, top_k=, as_of=, active_only=, tier=, max_hops=) / link / await traverse_graph / invalidate / promote / forget; SqliteMemoryStore(path); MemoryContextAdapter(memory, top_k=, tiers=, max_hops=); MemoryConsolidator(memory, registry=).consolidate(text, subject_id=). Tool pack: himmy.toolkit.memory.register_memory_pack(registry, ToolkitConfig) + effective_memory_path().
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk already wires register_memory_pack in agent_tools.py (durable remember/recall). To go further: enable memory_consolidate in ToolkitConfig to get a 'consolidate' tool (no duplicate facts), set memory_subject per-user, and register MemoryContextAdapter on the ContextService so the agent silently sees relevant prior facts every turn (no explicit recall tool-call). The HIMMY_MEMORY_PATH default is .himmy/memory.db under the working dir, so it is already durable. Use link()/max_hops to relate facts (paper->note->task) into a small knowledge graph; use as_of for 'what did I believe last month'.
capabilities.appOpportunity: A persistent assistant that remembers the user's projects, preferences, and prior conclusions across sessions; a 'what I knew as of <date>' time-travel view; and a related-facts panel that surfaces linked notes/papers/tasks for the current item — turning Himmy from a stateless palette into a memory that compounds.

capabilities.name: KNOWLEDGE BASE / RAG with durable SqliteKnowledgeBackend + hybrid retrieval + rerank + local embedders
capabilities.whatItDoes: KnowledgeBase chunks, embeds, and retrieves documents by cosine top-k with a parent-document context window, content-addressed ingest (re-running over an unchanged corpus is a no-op, changed sources replace not duplicate), embedder-fingerprint mismatch detection, and tenancy guards. Retrieval has a dense default and a RetrievalConfig(mode='hybrid') BM25+dense RRF fusion path with optional cross-encoder rerank and query rewrite. SqliteKnowledgeBackend is the offline/desktop twin of pgvector: it persists embeddings+chunks+fingerprint to a single .db file so an app RESTART loads the existing index instead of re-embedding the whole corpus (the docstring explicitly names the Daybook ~2-minute re-embed problem this solves). local_embedders.build_embedder('auto') gives genuine local semantic recall via fastembed (ONNX, no server) or a pulled Ollama embed model, degrading to the offline DeterministicEmbedder.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.services.knowledge: KnowledgeBase(storage=, embedder=, backend=, retrieval=RetrievalConfig(mode='hybrid')).create_kb / ingest_documents / ingest_file / ingest_directory / await search(kb_id, query, top_k=, metadata_filters=). SqliteKnowledgeBackend(path). retrieval.reranker.build_reranker()/fastembed_rerank_available(). local_embedders.build_embedder(name='auto'). Tool pack: himmy.toolkit.knowledge.register_knowledge_pack (kb_ingest/kb_search). KnowledgeBaseAdapter for context auto-injection.
capabilities.integrationNotes: papers_rag.py currently builds an in-memory KnowledgeBase(storage=StorageService(), retrieval=hybrid) and rebuilds it each launch, caching extracted PDF text on disk but re-embedding in memory. The highest-ROI change is to pass backend=SqliteKnowledgeBackend(<library_files>/papers_index.db) so the embeddings persist; content-hash dedup then skips re-embedding unchanged papers on the next launch. Also swap the DeterministicEmbedder for build_embedder('auto') so recall is genuinely semantic when fastembed/Ollama is present. KB_WORKSPACE_ID/KB_CLIENT_ID/KB_NAME are already set, so resolve_kb makes the durable KB stable by name.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Instant 'ask my library' on launch (no embedding spinner), reliably high-quality cited retrieval across a large paper collection, and a unified KB that can also index notes, saved news, and highlights — so the user can semantically search everything they have read, not just one corpus.

capabilities.name: GUARDRAILS / policy pipeline + DLP token vault
capabilities.whatItDoes: A composable, synchronous, dependency-free guardrail pipeline that inspects text on three surfaces (tool arguments, input prompt, model output) and returns a verdict that can redact, flag, or block, with per-guardrail attribution (GuardrailTrigger) safe to audit. Built-ins: PII redaction (incl. Nepal-specific), prompt-injection detection, blocklist, and a GroundingGuardrail. The DLP layer adds policy-driven detection with a reversible TokenVault (InMemory or SQLite-backed) so sensitive spans are tokenized out before the model sees them and re-substituted after — and pre/post tool hooks (build_guardrail_pre_hook/post_hook) plus suppresses_output_content() so a streaming caller knows to buffer-and-guard before emitting a blocked secret.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.services.guardrails: build_guardrail_pipeline(['pii','injection']).inspect(text) -> GuardrailVerdict(allowed, text, flags, triggers); GuardrailPipeline; build_guardrail_pre_hook/build_guardrail_post_hook(pipeline) for the tool service; build_dlp_guardrail(policy, vault); SqliteTokenVaultBackend(path); DlpPolicy/DlpAction; PresidioAnalyzerAdapter (optional).
capabilities.integrationNotes: Not used by scholar-desk today. Wire build_guardrail_pipeline into the /ask and /ask/stream paths in server.py: inspect the user prompt before the run and the model output after. Because the app streams (StreamingResponse), check pipeline.suppresses_output_content() to decide whether to buffer a turn before emitting tokens. Register build_guardrail_pre_hook on the ToolRegistry so connector tool args (e.g. outbound email/calendar writes) are redacted. The SqliteTokenVaultBackend keeps the redaction reversible and local — no cloud DLP service.
capabilities.appOpportunity: A privacy-conscious research desk that redacts personal info before any text leaves for a cloud model (important since himmy is text-only and forwards prompts to OpenRouter/Gemini), warns on prompt-injection from fetched web/PDF content, and gives the user a visible 'what was redacted' indicator — a genuine trust feature for a personal workspace.

capabilities.name: SELF-LEARNING (OUTCOME_SCORED + TOOL_FAILED -> reputation + reorder + trajectory hints)
capabilities.whatItDoes: An opt-in loop that turns recorded run-events into behavioural change. LearningService mines a bounded, workspace-scoped window of TOOL_COMPLETED/TOOL_FAILED into a per-tool operational reputation, and (with outcome_weight>0) blends in the mean of OUTCOME_SCORED grades. ToolReputationProvider snapshots this so ToolService.bound_tools stable-sorts flaky tools after reliable ones (annotate/reorder, never drop). LearnedHintsContextAdapter injects a short privacy-safe reliability note ('tool X failed 4 of its last 5 calls — verify inputs') into the prompt. TrajectoryAdvisor adds ordering/repetition-aware hints (looping, don't-call-first). OutcomeRecorder emits OUTCOME_SCORED from four provenance sources: an LLM judge, a deterministic grader, explicit user thumbs-up/down, or a trajectory grade. Everything is fail-open and inert unless spec.self_learning is on.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.services.learning: LearningService(event_log, window=, workspace_id=, outcome_weight=).get_tool_reputation(); ToolReputationProvider(learning, floor=, event_sink=).refresh()/score_for/is_unreliable; LearnedHintsContextAdapter(learning, trajectory_advisor=); OutcomeRecorder(...).record(score, source=OutcomeSource.USER_FEEDBACK); TrajectoryAdvisor. Spec flags: self_learning / outcome_weight / trajectory_hints in himmy/config/agent_spec.py; build_runtime_for_spec wires it all + exposes runtime.reprime_self_learning.
capabilities.integrationNotes: Set self_learning: true (and optionally outcome_weight + trajectory_hints) in scholar-desk's agent spec; build_runtime_for_spec then auto-wires LearningService + ToolReputationProvider + LearnedHintsContextAdapter against the same SqliteSessionStore/event log the app already uses (subject= the per-user workspace_id so reputation is per user). Then surface a thumbs-up/down in the Cmd-K answer UI and call OutcomeRecorder.record(..., source=USER_FEEDBACK) on click; with outcome_weight>0 those grades feed back into which tools the agent prefers.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Himmy quietly gets better at the user's workflow: tools/connectors that keep failing (a flaky web fetch, a rate-limited API) get deprioritized, repeated-call loops self-correct, and a thumbs-down on an answer measurably shifts future tool selection — a personal assistant that adapts to one user instead of staying static.

capabilities.name: MULTI-AGENT orchestrators (handoff/delegate teams, group-chat, StateGraph, planner)
capabilities.whatItDoes: Orchestration on top of the single-agent runtime. MultiAgentOrchestrator runs an AgentTeam of TeamMembers with two edge types: handoff (binds a synthetic transfer_to_<peer> tool, control moves to the peer on the same thread — swarm routing) and delegate (binds ask_<worker> that runs a worker to completion in a sub-thread and returns its answer — supervisor pattern), with fail-closed HITL handling. GroupChatOrchestrator runs round-robin/LLM-selected multi-speaker discussions with termination predicates and a fan_out helper. StateGraph is a LangGraph-style typed-state graph with reducers, interrupts/resume, and recursion guards. PlannerOrchestrator does plan-and-execute; reflect() does a critique-and-revise pass.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.orchestrators: MultiAgentOrchestrator + AgentTeam(members=[TeamMember(persona, tools, handoffs=, delegates=)]); GroupChatOrchestrator + RoundRobinSelector/LLMSelector + fan_out; StateGraph/CompiledStateGraph + START/END/add_reducer + NodeInterrupt; PlannerOrchestrator(runtime, max_steps=).run(); reflect(runtime, draft, criteria=).
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk already uses PlannerOrchestrator + reflect in research.py deep_research. The next step is a research COUNCIL: a MultiAgentOrchestrator team (e.g. a 'searcher' delegate that runs ask_papers/web search, an 'analyst' that synthesizes, a 'critic' worker) rather than one planner loop — wire it with the same _build_runtime from cli.py. For long multi-stage reports, StateGraph gives durable interrupt/resume so a partial report survives an app restart (it already wires SqliteCheckpointStore).
capabilities.appOpportunity: A 'Deep Research' mode that fans a question across specialist sub-agents (gather -> draft -> critique -> revise) and shows the user the intermediate reasoning steps, plus resumable long-running report generation that can be paused and continued across sessions — a meaningfully more capable research product than a single Q&A turn.

capabilities.name: EVAL + BFCL-grade benchmark harness
capabilities.whatItDoes: A reproducible benchmark runner that executes a YAML suite (BFCL/core/irrelevance/multiagent/nepali/skills) of tasks against one or more model specs over N trials in isolated throwaway workspaces, running the real agent loop and grading correctness (graders incl. arg-level/data-flow trajectory grading via tool_called_with/result_feeds_arg/all_of/any_of), whether the expected tool was called, latency, turns, tokens, and cost. Includes an LLM judge with a judge!=candidate guard, statistics (McNemar/Holm via stats.py), a history.jsonl trend log, and a leaderboard report. The evaluation service adds AgentEvalHarness.evaluate_agent/evaluate_team for grading a configured agent against datasets, plus privacy/metrics.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy CLI `himmy bench` (cli/commands.py cmd_bench). himmy.benchmark: BenchmarkRunner(trials=, inference_factory=, judge_inference_factory=).run(suite, specs); graders.grade/grade_trajectory; judge.build_judge/judge_answer; history; report; stats. himmy.services.evaluation.agent_harness.AgentEvalHarness.evaluate_agent/evaluate_team.
capabilities.integrationNotes: Not used by scholar-desk. Author a small in-repo YAML suite of representative research-desk tasks (ask_papers retrieval, citation formatting, calendar/gmail tool selection, 'should NOT call a tool' irrelevance cases) and run BenchmarkRunner with an inference_factory pointing at the app's configured local/OpenRouter model. Use it as a regression gate when changing the agent spec, prompts, or tool set, and to compare candidate local models (which Ollama/embed model to ship).
capabilities.appOpportunity: Confidence that a prompt/model/tool change actually improves the assistant before shipping it to the user — and a principled way to pick the smallest local model that still answers research questions well, which matters for an offline-first desktop app.

capabilities.name: CONNECTORS framework (connector-as-tool: SDK + declarative YAML spec)
capabilities.whatItDoes: A secure pattern for bridging the agent to external systems. OutboundToolConnector exposes typed tools whose credentials come from the secrets layer (never literals), whose HTTP is SSRF-guarded via a pinned transport, which are capability-gated, audited (one structured event per action), rate-limited (token bucket), retry-bounded, and idempotent (dedupe on retry) — and tools can be marked requires_approval for human-in-the-loop on side effects. register_tools(registry) binds them as native local tools. ConnectorSpec lets a whole REST connector (tools, auth, egress, rate limit, per-tool approval/idempotency) be declared in YAML with no Python. InboundChannelConnector handles receive-message->run-agent->reply with default-deny sender allowlists + HMAC/signature verification. Built-ins: GitHub, Slack, Discord, web search (Tavily/Brave), Nepal news, NRB, generic signed webhook. A ConnectorService catalogs/configures/tests/enables connectors.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.connectors: OutboundToolConnector subclass with @capability + .register_tools(registry); ConnectorSpec/register_connector_specs (YAML); ConnectorRegistry/register_connector/default_registry; ConnectorContext/RateLimiter/RetryPolicy/IdempotencyStore; capability/redact_args/safe_error/verify_hmac_signature; ConnectorService(catalog/configure/enable/test/build); register_websearch_connectors/register_github_connectors etc.
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk hand-rolls its connectors (PapersRagConnector, GoogleCalendarConnector, ActionsConnector) with a safe_register_local_tool helper (to dodge the durable-content-addressing re-register bug noted in memory), bypassing the SDK. Migrating side-effecting tools (gmail_send, gcal_create, outbound webhooks) onto OutboundToolConnector would give them SSRF guarding, secret resolution, rate limiting, idempotency, and requires_approval HITL for free. For new third-party REST integrations (e.g. Semantic Scholar, arXiv, Crossref already used in library.py), a ConnectorSpec YAML is the lowest-effort path. The ConnectorService catalog could back a Settings 'Connections' UI.
capabilities.appOpportunity: A trustworthy 'Connections' settings panel where the user enables Gmail/Calendar/web-search/paper-APIs with per-connector status and test buttons, side-effecting actions (send email, create event) prompt for confirmation (HITL), and credentials are stored safely — turning ad-hoc integrations into a managed, auditable surface.

capabilities.name: Structured / constrained output + streaming
capabilities.whatItDoes: The inference kernel can guarantee structured output: a JSON-Schema is compiled into the constraint each backend needs so the model physically cannot emit invalid output (Ollama format pass-through, llama.cpp GBNF grammar via schema_to_gbnf, OpenAI/OpenRouter response_format json_schema/guided tool-args), opt-in via the CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY request-metadata flag and fail-open (an unsupported schema widens to permissive JSON rather than breaking). ResponseFormat (AUTO_TOOLS etc.) on a run controls tool/JSON behaviour. The runtime exposes true token streaming: stream_task / stream_agent_loop yield incremental text over InferenceService.run_stream, with audit parity to the non-streaming path.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.services.inference.structured: constrained_decoding_requested(request), schema_to_gbnf(schema), ollama_format(schema), CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY. himmy.services.inference.models: ResponseFormat, LLMConfig, InferenceRequest(metadata={'constrained_decoding':...}, output_json_schema=). SingleAgentRuntime.stream_task / stream_agent_loop / run_task_detailed (returns typed RunResult with structured output, cost, tool_calls).
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk already streams (server.py /ask/stream via answer_stream->stream_task) and already uses ResponseFormat.AUTO_TOOLS in research.py. The unused lever is constrained decoding: for features that need machine-parseable output (extracting structured metadata from a PDF, generating a citation object, producing a typed summary card), pass output_json_schema + metadata={'constrained_decoding': true} on the run so the JSON is guaranteed valid instead of parsed-and-hoped. Pure/offline and fail-open, so it is safe to add behind a flag.
capabilities.appOpportunity: Reliable structured features — auto-extract title/authors/venue/DOI from a dropped PDF into a fillable form, generate citation objects that always parse, and render typed 'answer cards' (summary + sources + confidence) — without brittle regex-on-LLM-prose, making the productivity surfaces dependable.

capabilities.name: Sessions / threads / audit (EntityRecord spine + run-event stream)
capabilities.whatItDoes: The framework's audit spine: immutable content-addressed EntityRecords with deterministic UUID5 ids (stable_id_for/record_id_for), typed EntityLinks, an EntityRegistry (SQLite or Postgres backends) with metadata-containment queries mirroring Postgres JSONB @>, integrity/lineage helpers, and projection so subsystems (memory, learning) live ON the spine. On top sit durable conversation state: SqliteSessionStore (persistent threads/messages), SqliteCheckpointStore (resumable runs with redact_sensitive_args), and the RunEvent stream (TOOL_CALLED/COMPLETED/FAILED, OUTCOME_SCORED, MEMORY_*, LEARNING_APPLIED) that the learning + observability layers read. build_runtime_for_spec assembles the whole thing; an observability metrics registry tracks tokens/cost.
capabilities.publicApi: himmy.entities: EntityRecord/EntityLink, EntityRegistry, SqliteEntityRegistry, stable_id_for/record_id_for, lineage/integrity. himmy.runtime.session.SqliteSessionStore; himmy.runtime.checkpoint.SqliteCheckpointStore + redact_sensitive_args; himmy.config.project.conversations_db_path. himmy.core.events RunEvent/EventType/EventSink. himmy.services.observability.metrics.get_registry.
capabilities.integrationNotes: scholar-desk already uses SqliteSessionStore (cli.py conversations) + SqliteCheckpointStore and reads the observability metrics registry for token/cost usage (server.py /usage). The under-used piece is the RunEvent stream + EntityRegistry: passing a registry into MemoryService and turning on self_learning would make every tool call, memory write, and learning decision an auditable spine record (the same backend the session store uses), enabling a verifiable activity/provenance log. EntityLinks could relate a note to the paper and run it was derived from.
capabilities.appOpportunity: An 'Activity & Provenance' view: a trustworthy timeline of what Himmy did (tools called, what it read, what it remembered, why it chose an answer) with per-answer source links — plus resumable runs and accurate per-session token/cost accounting, which a power-user research desktop benefits from for both transparency and debugging.

appBackend.area: Daybook/Himmy desktop app — backend + agent surface
appBackend.summary: Himmy is a single-user local macOS workspace: an Electron shell (desktop/electron/main.cjs spawns the Python backend as a child process and kills it on quit) over a FastAPI server (src/himmy_app/server.py, binds 127.0.0.1:8131, wide-open CORS, no auth). The backend wraps a himmy-framework agent (agent/agent.yaml, provider=openrouter google/gemini-2.5-flash) plus several "direct, no-agent-in-the-loop" feature modules: a native reference-manager Library (library.py), a deterministic News hub (news.py), a papers RAG index (connectors/papers_rag.py), deep research (research.py), tasks/google via himmy's own packs, and chat (cli.py). ALL durable data lives under .scholar-desk/ (a folder kept named ".scholar-desk" for data continuity even though the product is "Himmy"). The agent currently has 18 tools across utils/web/memory/tasks/google/RAG/calendar/actions. The single biggest gap across the whole surface is automation/scheduling: there is NO scheduler, worker, cron, timer, OS-notification, digest, reminder, or due-date firing anywhere — tasks have a `due` column that nothing acts on, news has no refresh loop, calendar/mail are poll-on-open only, and approval-gated runs only resume on an explicit UI call. Frontend setInterval calls are pure UI refresh polling, not backend automation.
notableFindings: NO scheduling/automation layer exists at all. grep for schedul/cron/worker/routine/timer/reminder/notify/digest across src/himmy_app/ returns only unrelated hits (research.py 'worker threads', a reverted RAG 'warm' comment). The Electron main process (desktop/electron/main.cjs, 136 lines) only spawns/kills the backend and has no Notification/powerMonitor/setInterval. This is the clean slate for any proposed scheduling feature.

Tasks have a `due` field (server.py _task_dict returns t.due; himmy's studio_tasks store) but NOTHING reads or fires on it — no overdue detection, no reminders, no calendar linkage. add_task tool only accepts a `title`, so the agent cannot even set a due date.

Mail is READ-ONLY by design and Calendar WRITE + mail_send are HITL approval-gated. The HITL machinery (approvals.db SqliteCheckpointStore, /ask/resume, ask_turn/resume_turn) is real and working, but a paused run only continues when the UI explicitly POSTs /ask/resume — there is no timeout, expiry, or auto-resume.

Two tool sets overlap on Google Calendar: himmy's built-in `google` pack (gcal_events/gcal_create, registered but mostly not in the allowlist) AND the project's GoogleCalendarConnector (calendar_find/add/edit/remove). The agent.yaml allowlist admits the connector's 4 + gmail_inbox only; gmail_send from the pack is deliberately excluded in favor of the approval-gated mail_send action tool.

RAG index is in-memory, rebuilt lazily on first ask_papers and on any change to the library item-id set; a startup 'warm' was tried and reverted (GIL froze the loop), so first-run cold-build (~2-3 min over all PDFs) is a known cost. Index is NOT persisted across restarts (only extracted PDF text is cached in papers_cache.db).

Zotero is fully deprecated: config.py still carries zotero_api_base/library and connectors/zotero.py + zotero_client.py still exist and are exported from __init__.py, but agent_tools.register never wires ZoteroConnector — Himmy reads its own library.db directly. Dead/legacy surface.

Usage/cost accounting (/usage) folds himmy's process-wide metrics-registry counters into a persisted lifetime tally in .scholar-desk/usage.json, surviving backend restarts by detecting counter resets.

Streaming (/ask/stream) does NOT truly stream the tool loop: it runs the bounded HITL tool loop buffered, then re-issues one no-tool synthesis turn and re-chunks the guard-cleared reply into word-group deltas for a 'premium reveal' feel — because the blocking output guardrail (grounding/PII/injection) cannot stream past content it might withhold.
Self-learning (TOOL_FAILED reputation priming) and context compaction are ON in agent.yaml; the self-learning prime is scheduled as a background task on the running loop to avoid crashing /ask.

Deep research (/research) bypasses the LLM's tool-choice for the library leg: it calls the RAG retriever directly in Python (deterministic grounding) and runs the web leg best-effort in a thread; web search is keyless DuckDuckGo and frequently empty without HIMMY_SEARCH_API_KEY (tavily/brave).

surfaces.name: Chat / Ask + Streaming (Cmd-K assistant)
surfaces.description: The reactive single-turn agent loop powering the command-palette assistant. POST /ask (one turn, HITL on, returns reply+tools or an awaiting_approval checkpoint), POST /ask/resume (approve/reject a gated tool and continue), POST /ask/stream (SSE token deltas via a buffered-then-re-chunked synthesis turn). Context can include the currently-viewed paper/article.
surfaces.currentState: Working. HITL approval-gating real (approvals.db). Streaming is simulated (buffered tool loop + word-group re-chunk) because of the blocking output guardrail. Self-learning + context compaction enabled.
gaps: No true token streaming through the guardrail.
Approval-gated runs never time out / auto-expire; resume only on explicit UI POST.
No per-turn server-side error surfacing beyond a string reply.

surfaces.name: Sessions (persistent conversations / history sidebar)
surfaces.description: Durable Cmd-K conversation store. GET /sessions (list w/ title preview), GET /sessions/{id} (messages), DELETE /sessions/{id}. Threads are himmy ChatThreads persisted to conversations.db; synthesis-plumbing turns are stripped from the displayed transcript.
surfaces.currentState: Working. Pinned to .scholar-desk/conversations.db via HIMMY_CONVERSATIONS_PATH so all endpoints share one DB regardless of cwd.
gaps: delete_session reaches into store._store (private API).
No rename, search, pinning, or export of conversations.
The streaming path always writes to session id 'last' when none given (can collide).

surfaces.name: Library (native reference manager)
surfaces.description: Himmy's own paper store — SQLite catalogue library.db + PDF store library_files/. Add by DOI (Crossref) or arXiv (arXiv API) or import PDFs; open-access PDF fetch via Unpaywall/arXiv; AI-assisted enrich (model reads page 1 -> Crossref); metadata edit; per-item notes; delete; dedupe (annotation-protected). Endpoints: GET /library, GET /library/{id}, GET /library/{id}/pdf, POST /library/doi, POST /library/files, PUT /library/{id}, POST /library/{id}/enrich, POST /library/{id}/fetch-pdf, PUT /library/{id}/notes, DELETE /library/{id}, POST /library/dedupe, GET /datadir.
surfaces.currentState: Working and feature-rich (described in memory as Zotero-grade, E2E-tested).
gaps: No full-text search inside the library tab (list() filters title/author/venue/tags only; full-text search is only via RAG).
enrich depends on OPENROUTER_API_KEY; silently returns {} if absent.
add_files only accepts local paths (no multipart upload endpoint).
No BibTeX/RIS import; citation export not exposed as an endpoint here (only highlights markdown export).

surfaces.name: Collections & Tags
surfaces.description: Folders + membership and tag aggregation. GET/POST /collections, PUT/DELETE /collections/{cid}, POST/DELETE /collections/{cid}/items/{item_id}, GET /tags. Tags are a JSON column on items updated via PUT /library/{id}.
surfaces.currentState: Working.
gaps: Tags edited only through the generic item-update path (no dedicated add/remove-tag endpoint).
No nested collections / smart collections / saved searches.

surfaces.name: Highlights & Annotations
surfaces.description: PDF highlights/notes stored in the highlights table (page, color, text, note, rects JSON). GET/POST /library/{id}/highlights, PUT/DELETE /highlights/{hid}, POST /library/{id}/highlights/export (writes a Markdown file of notes+highlights to ~/Downloads).
surfaces.currentState: Working.
gaps: Export only to ~/Downloads markdown (no clipboard/return-body variant, no per-collection export).
Highlights not surfaced to the RAG/agent (only the item-level note is).

surfaces.name: News hub (feeds + reader + saved articles)
surfaces.description: Deterministic, non-LLM RSS reader. Categories Nepal/World/Business/Technology + a personalised 'For You' built from saved interests via Google News RSS. Endpoints: GET/PUT /news/interests, GET /news/categories, GET /news/feed?cat&force, GET /news/recommendations (arXiv paper recs from interests), GET /news/article?url (Safari-Reader extraction via trafilatura/bs4). Saved articles (folders) stored in library.db saved_news table and fed to RAG: GET /news/saved/folders, /news/saved/urls, /news/saved, /news/saved/{nid}, POST /news/save, PUT /news/saved/{nid} (move), DELETE /news/saved/{nid}.
surfaces.currentState: Working. 15-min in-memory/file cache (news_cache.json). Interests in news.json.
gaps: No background refresh — feeds only update when a client requests them (or force=true); no scheduled morning digest/notification.
RSS parsing is regex-based (fragile).
'Recommended papers' surface returns up to 8 arXiv candidates but has no save-to-library shortcut endpoint and no dedup against existing library.

surfaces.name: Tasks board
surfaces.description: Shared task board over himmy's studio_tasks SQLite store, pinned to .scholar-desk/tasks.db (HIMMY_TASKS_PATH) so the agent's tools and the UI share one DB. GET /tasks (with open/total counts), POST /tasks (title), POST /tasks/{id}/complete, DELETE /tasks/{id}. Same store the agent's list_tasks/add_task/complete_task hit.
surfaces.currentState: Working for create/list/complete/delete.
gaps: `due` field is read/returned but NEVER set (add takes only title) and NEVER acted on — no reminders, overdue flags, or notifications.
No edit/reschedule, no priority/tags, no subtasks, no recurring tasks.
No calendar integration (tasks and calendar are entirely separate).
No automation: nothing watches the board.

surfaces.name: Google Calendar + Mail
surfaces.description: Read-only Gmail + read/write Calendar over himmy.api.studio_google with a loopback OAuth2 flow. Setup/auth: GET /google/status, POST /google/client, GET /google/auth-url, POST /google/exchange, GET /google/callback (renders a close-tab page), POST /google/disconnect. Data: GET /mail/inbox, GET /calendar/events, GET /calendar/range (month grid), POST /calendar/events, PUT/DELETE /calendar/events/{id}. Tokens persisted in keychain (HIMMY_SECRETS=keychain).
surfaces.currentState: Working. Mail strictly read-only (no send endpoint). Calendar full CRUD via REST; agent calendar writes are HITL-gated.
gaps: Mail is read-only at the REST layer; sending only via the agent's approval-gated mail_send tool (no /mail/send endpoint, no compose UI path documented here).
No mail labels/threads/search; inbox capped at 30.
No background sync/notification of new mail or upcoming events — fetched only when a tab is opened/polled.
User must self-provision a Google OAuth client (client_id/secret) — non-trivial for a non-technical user.
No free/busy or scheduling-assistant logic beyond raw event CRUD.

surfaces.name: Deep Research
surfaces.description: Explicit multi-step orchestration (plan -> fan-out library+web concurrently -> synthesize cited brief -> reflect). POST /research {question}. Library leg calls the RAG retriever directly in Python (deterministic grounding); web leg is best-effort in a thread. Returns {ok, brief, sources, steps}. Bounded by a 180s wall-clock deadline.
surfaces.currentState: Working; never automatic (UI button only).
gaps: Web leg uses keyless DuckDuckGo and is usually empty without HIMMY_SEARCH_API_KEY (tavily/brave).
No streaming/progress events to the UI during the run (returns only at the end).
No persistence of past research briefs (not saved anywhere).

surfaces.name: Index / Papers RAG
surfaces.description: Hybrid (BM25+dense) himmy KnowledgeBase over library papers + saved news, with an optional fastembed cross-encoder reranker (probed once, degrades to plain hybrid). POST /index {force} rebuilds; the index also auto-refreshes when the library item-id set changes. Extracted PDF text cached in papers_cache.db. Exposed to the agent as ask_papers/index_papers.
surfaces.currentState: Working but in-memory only.
gaps: Index not persisted across restarts (only PDF text cache is) -> cold rebuild on every backend start; startup warm was reverted (GIL).
Refresh trigger only watches the SET of item ids, not content edits (re-enriched metadata won't re-index unless ids change or force=true).
In-memory search is sync inside the loop for parts of the KB (per memory notes); large libraries risk a slow first build (~2-3 min).

surfaces.name: Backup / Restore
surfaces.description: Whole-library zip (catalogue + PDFs) for cross-device sync via a cloud folder. POST /backup (writes himmy-backup-<ts>.zip to ~/Downloads), POST /restore {path}, GET /datadir.
surfaces.currentState: Working (manual).
gaps: Backs up only library.db + library_files/ — NOT highlights/collections are in library.db so OK, but conversations.db, memory.db, tasks.db, news.json, saved_news (in library.db, OK), usage.json are NOT included.
Backup destination hardcoded to ~/Downloads; no scheduled/auto backup.
Restore is destructive (replaces library wholesale).

surfaces.name: Agent tools (the agent's hands)
surfaces.description: 18 tools registered by himmy_app.agent_tools:register and allowlisted in agent.yaml. From himmy built-in packs: calculator, current_time (utils); web_search, web_fetch (web); list_tasks, add_task, complete_task (tasks, shared SQLite); gmail_inbox (google, read-only); remember, recall (memory pack, durable). From the papers_rag connector: ask_papers, index_papers. From GoogleCalendarConnector: calendar_find, calendar_add (gated), calendar_edit (gated), calendar_remove (gated). From ActionsConnector: save_article (direct), add_paper (direct), mail_send (approval-gated). Registration is resilient via safe_register_local_tool (works around himmy content-address violations that previously dropped calendar_edit/remove).
surfaces.currentState: Working. 4 approval-gated tools (calendar add/edit/remove + mail_send). Zotero connector exists but is NOT registered.
gaps: add_task tool takes only `title` — agent cannot set due dates, so no task-time awareness.
No tool to schedule anything, set a reminder, or create a recurring background job — the agent cannot set up automation for itself.
gmail_send registered by the google pack but excluded from the allowlist (only the gated mail_send action is exposed).
Two overlapping calendar tool families (built-in gcal_* vs connector calendar_*); only the connector set is in the allowlist.

surfaces.name: Persisted data (.scholar-desk data dir)
surfaces.description: All durable state under .scholar-desk/ (DEFAULT_DATA_DIR, override HIMMY_APP_DATA_DIR). library.db (items, highlights, collections, item_collections, saved_news tables) + library_files/ (PDFs); papers_cache.db (extracted PDF text for RAG); conversations.db (Cmd-K chat threads); memory.db (durable remember/recall, subject 'scholar-desk', fastembed); tasks.db (shared task board); approvals.db (HITL checkpoints); storage.db (himmy durable entity/audit spine); news.json (interests) + news_cache.json (feed cache); usage.json (lifetime token/cost tally). Secrets (Google OAuth client+tokens) in macOS keychain, not on disk.
surfaces.currentState: All present and in active use (confirmed on disk).
gaps: Folder named '.scholar-desk' (legacy) intentionally kept for data continuity despite the 'Himmy' rebrand.
No scheduling/state store exists (no jobs/routines table) — a scheduling feature would need a new persisted store here.
Backup only covers library.db + PDFs, leaving conversations/memory/tasks/usage unprotected.

surfaces.name: Scheduling / Automation (ABSENT — gap surface)
surfaces.description: What a 'set it and forget it' layer WOULD cover: timed news digests, morning briefings, scheduled backups, task due-date reminders, recurring research runs, auto-index, new-mail/event notifications, auto-expiry of stale approval checkpoints.
surfaces.currentState: Does not exist. No scheduler, worker, cron bridge, asyncio background loop, OS notification, or due-date firing anywhere in the backend or Electron main process. Frontend setInterval calls (App.tsx) are pure UI refresh polling (tasks 5s, usage 15s, clock). Electron main.cjs (136 lines) only spawns/kills the backend.
gaps: No scheduler/worker process (note: himmy framework ships a Routines layer per user memory, but it is NOT wired into this app).
No native macOS notifications (Electron Notification/powerMonitor unused).
No task due-date reminders despite a `due` column.
No scheduled news digest or auto-refresh.
No scheduled/auto backups.
No approval-checkpoint timeout/expiry.
Backend only runs while the Electron app is open (child process killed on quit) — any future scheduling must account for the app not always being running.

appFrontend.area: Himmy (Daybook) desktop app — Electron + React frontend
appFrontend.summary: The frontend lives in /Users/samriddhagc/LocalProjects/scholar-desk/desktop. It is a native-feeling macOS Electron app (vibrancy/frosted-glass, hidden-inset titlebar) whose renderer is an almost-monolithic React + TypeScript + Tailwind SPA. electron/main.cjs spawns the Python himmy backend (python -m himmy_app.server on port 8131, env HIMMY_APP_PORT) and opens ONE BrowserWindow (1340x880, vibrancy under-window, titleBarStyle hiddenInset, traffic-light inset) onto the Vite dev server (localhost:5173) or the packaged dist/index.html. preload.cjs exposes a small window.himmy bridge: backendPort, platform, pickPapers (native PDF open dialog), pathForFile (resolve dropped File to path), revealData (open .scholar-desk in Finder), pickZip (restore backup), openExternal (Google OAuth opens in system browser). The renderer talks to the backend purely over HTTP via src/lib/api.ts (fetch to http://127.0.0.1:8131; SSE for chat streaming with auto-fallback to /ask). Almost the entire UI is ONE file: src/App.tsx (3,059 lines — every surface + the command palette), with the PDF reader split into src/Reader.tsx (473 lines) and citation formatters in src/lib/cite.ts. There is NO router library — navigation is a single `section` useState in App() (type Section = today|news|library|planner|tasks|calendar|mail) plus two custom window events used as a global bus: `himmy:nav` (jump to a section from any card) and `himmy:ask` (open the assistant pre-filled). A second in-module `_refreshBus` (App.tsx:38-73, surfaces calendar|tasks|library|news) lets the assistant trigger live re-fetch of an open view after it runs a write tool (emitRefreshForTools maps tool names to surfaces). An openItemRef module ref (App.tsx:79-119) tracks the currently-open paper/article so the assistant gets it as context. Design language = macOS label system: white-with-opacity ink tiers (mac.ink/ink2/ink3/ink4), translucent fills over vibrancy (mac.fill/fillHi/stroke/strokeHi), one system-blue accent (#0A84FF / accentHi #409CFF), status colors (green #30D158, red #FF453A, orange #FF9F0A), SF Pro Text/Display fonts, 10px radii (rounded-mac), custom shadows (mac/pop/tab), 13px base. CSS in src/index.css adds a 0.62-alpha dark tint over the native vibrancy, custom scrollbars, blue focus ring, and keyframe animations (himmy-pop center-enter, himmy-slide-right dock-enter, himmy-bubble-in iMessage bubble, himmy-typing dots). lucide-react is the icon set; @blueprintjs/core+icons are dependencies but are effectively unused in App.tsx (no Blueprint components imported there).

surfaces.name: App shell / Toolbar + Content router
surfaces.description: Root layout. A 52px draggable titlebar (Toolbar, App.tsx:180) with a centered pill nav (Today/News/Library/Planner/Mail driven by the NAV array App.tsx:26) and a right-side Settings gear; a flex-1 main area that either shows the Reader (when openId set) or routes a surface via Content's switch(section) (App.tsx:216); the global CommandBar; and the conditional SettingsPanel modal. Health is polled every 5s (api.health) to drive an online/offline state.
surfaces.currentState: Complete and working. 5 visible nav tabs; Planner expands to tasks/calendar sub-sections. Online dot logic exists but the toolbar currently renders no visible status indicator (online prop computed but unused in the header JSX).
gaps: NAV array (App.tsx:26-32) is the single source of truth for top-level tabs — adding a new top-level surface = add an entry here + a case in Content (App.tsx:218-226) + the Section union type (App.tsx:25).
No persistent left sidebar at the shell level (only the Library has its own rail); a settings/preferences area is only a modal, not a full surface.
online/offline health is computed but not surfaced visually.

surfaces.name: Today (home cockpit)
surfaces.description: Default landing surface (Today, App.tsx:256). A time-of-day hero (greeting + live clock + date), then a 12-col grid of cards: 'Up next' (next calendar events, gated on Google connect), 'To do' (top open tasks with inline add + hover-to-complete), 'Jump back in' (recent papers + saved articles), and a full-width 'Usage' card (UsageCard, App.tsx:518 — token + cost meter for the assistant, session vs all-time, polled every 15s from /usage). Cards subscribe to the refresh bus so assistant mutations show instantly.
surfaces.currentState: Complete and polished. Pulls tasks (5s poll), saved reading, library, calendar, and usage live.
gaps: This is the most natural home for new at-a-glance widgets (e.g. a 'Reminders / Schedules' card, a 'Today's agenda' digest). New cards drop straight into the grid in Today() around App.tsx:340-417 using the shared Card component (App.tsx:2422).
There is an unused Step component (App.tsx:567) that looks built for a first-run onboarding/setup checklist but is not rendered anywhere — a ready-made pattern for a getting-started card.
Greeting/clock use local Date only; no settings for locale/timezone.

surfaces.name: Library (reference manager)
surfaces.description: Zotero-grade library (Library, App.tsx:593). Left CollectionsRail (App.tsx:907) lists All / collections / tags / a 'Recommended' entry; main area is a PaperTable (App.tsx:1049, draggable rows for drag-into-collection) with search, 'Add by DOI/arXiv' inline field, native 'Add papers' file picker, drag-and-drop PDF zone (EmptyLibrary App.tsx:1093), and a 'Cite' export modal (CiteExport App.tsx:856 — APA/MLA/BibTeX via lib/cite.ts). 'Recommended' swaps the main pane to RecommendedMain (App.tsx:796) showing arXiv suggestions from saved research interests (api.news.recommendations / interests).
surfaces.currentState: Complete, the richest surface. Collections, tags, search, add-by-DOI, drag-drop ingest, citation export, and recommendations all wired.
gaps: api.index(force) (RAG re-index endpoint, api.ts:223) exists but is NOT wired to any UI control — there is no 'rebuild knowledge base / re-index' button anywhere in the renderer.
No bulk-select / multi-paper actions (cite/export operate on the whole filtered list, not a selection).

surfaces.name: Reader (PDF reader + metadata/notes/highlights)
surfaces.description: Full-screen reader (Reader.tsx) shown when a paper is opened (App renders <Reader> in place of Content). Lazy pdf.js rendering with a selectable text layer and a separate highlight overlay layer; 4 highlight colors; floating highlight popover. Right panel has 3 tabs (Reader.tsx:267-274): Info (InfoPanel — editable metadata via api.library.update, enrich via api.library.enrich, TagsEditor), Notes (NotesPanel — per-paper notes), Highlights (list + per-highlight note/color edit + delete + export to markdown via api.highlights.exportMarkdown). Toolbar: back, zoom, cite, fetch-PDF when missing. Sets openItemRef so the assistant knows 'this paper'.
surfaces.currentState: Complete and E2E-tested per project memory. Highlights, notes, metadata edit, enrich, fetch-PDF, citation, markdown export all functional.
gaps: pdf.worker is bundled via Vite ?url import; depends on pdfjs-dist version.
No table-of-contents/outline or page thumbnails navigation.

surfaces.name: Planner — Tasks
surfaces.description: Tasks view (Tasks, App.tsx:1725), reachable via the Planner tab's Tasks sub-tab or directly via section='tasks'. Add-a-task input (Enter to add), open tasks newest-first with completed sinking to the bottom, hover-to-complete and delete (TaskRow App.tsx:1819). Polls /tasks every 4s and subscribes to the 'tasks' refresh bus so assistant-added tasks (add_task / complete_task tools) appear live.
surfaces.currentState: Complete but minimal. Tasks are title + done + optional free-text due + created_at (api.ts Task type); no editing, no structured due-date picker, no recurrence.
gaps: Tasks `due` is free text (Himmy stores 'tomorrow'/dates as strings); isOverdue (App.tsx:559) is best-effort parse. No real due-date UI or reminders.

This is the natural sibling location for a Reminders view or a Schedules/Routines panel — Planner already has a sub-tab switcher (Planner App.tsx:1408) so adding a third sub-tab (e.g. 'Reminders' or 'Routines') is mechanically trivial: add to the tab array at App.tsx:1415 and render a new component. Or add 'planner'-style entries (tasks/calendar already map to Section).

surfaces.name: Planner — Calendar (Google, read+write)
surfaces.description: Calendar view (CalendarTab, App.tsx:1432), Planner's Calendar sub-tab. Month grid (MonthGrid App.tsx:1578) and list view; create/edit/delete events via an EventEditor modal (App.tsx:1620) that writes through api.calendar.create/update/remove (sends local wall-clock; backend attaches timezone). Gated behind Google connect — shows GoogleConnect (App.tsx:1233) until an account is linked. Subscribes to the 'calendar' refresh bus so assistant calendar_add/edit/remove updates the grid live.
surfaces.currentState: Complete. Full month/list, CRUD events, recurring-event id awareness. Read+write to the connected Google Calendar.
gaps: Week/day views not present (only month + list).
All calendar functionality requires Google connect; no local-only calendar.

surfaces.name: Mail (Google inbox, read-only)
surfaces.description: MailTab (App.tsx:1319), top-level nav tab. Read-only Gmail inbox list (MailRow App.tsx:1366) via api.mail.inbox. Gated behind Google connect (shares the useGoogle hook App.tsx:1141 with Calendar); shows GoogleConnect / GoogleSetup (one-time OAuth client_id/secret entry, GoogleSetup App.tsx:1183) until linked. GoogleTabHeader (App.tsx:1292) shows the account email + Refresh + Disconnect.
surfaces.currentState: Complete but intentionally read-only — Himmy can see inbox + calendar, never send/modify mail.
gaps: No message detail/open view (just sender/subject/snippet/date rows); no compose (read-only by design).
Google OAuth requires the user to paste their own Cloud client_id/secret (GoogleSetup) — a friction point, but the only place credentials are entered in-app.

surfaces.name: News (feeds + in-app reader + saved → RAG)
surfaces.description: News surface (News, App.tsx:1850), top-level nav tab. Left NewsRail (App.tsx:2046) toggles between feed categories (For You / Nepal / World / Business / Technology, NEWS_FEEDS App.tsx:1842) and saved folders. Cards (NewsCard App.tsx:2113, SavedCard App.tsx:2141) with images; in-app readable-article reader (NewsReader App.tsx:2272, serif typography) via api.news.article; save/unsave into folders (FolderMenu App.tsx:2243); 'For You' needs interests (interest editor inline). Saved articles feed the assistant's RAG. Sets openItemRef so Himmy knows 'this article'. Subscribes to 'news' refresh bus.
surfaces.currentState: Complete. Live feeds, in-app reader, save-to-folder, interests, recommendations all wired.
gaps: News interests are shared with Library 'Recommended' (same /news/interests endpoint) — a settings/preferences surface would be the cleaner home for managing interests centrally.

surfaces.name: Himmy assistant — ⌘K command palette / chat
surfaces.description: CommandBar (App.tsx:2480) is the global assistant, summoned by ⌘K (or himmy:ask events from any 'Ask Himmy' button). Not a nav tab — overlays everything. Modes: floating center modal OR docked right side-panel (DOCK_KEY localStorage, toggle App.tsx:2683), plus a minimized floating pill. iMessage-style streaming chat bubbles (Bubble App.tsx:2882, TypingDots App.tsx:2872) over SSE (api.askStream); starter CHIPS (App.tsx:2465); conversation history sidebar (api.sessions, persisted session id in localStorage daybook.chat.session); per-turn context injection (buildAskContext sends the open paper/article); a tool-approval flow (ApprovalCard App.tsx:2951 — approve/cancel gated tools via api.resume); and an explicit Deep research button (Telescope, ResearchLoading App.tsx:2794 / ResearchCard App.tsx:2817 via api.research, 60-150s).
surfaces.currentState: Complete and central to the product. Streaming, sessions/history, dock/float/minimize, HITL tool approvals, deep research, and live-refresh-after-tool all work.
gaps: No model/provider selector in the UI (model shown read-only on the Today Usage card from /usage).
The assistant is the primary entry point for scheduling/reminders today (tasks/calendar via tools) — a dedicated Schedules/Routines surface would complement, not replace, it.

surfaces.name: Settings (Backup & Sync modal)
surfaces.description: SettingsPanel (App.tsx:970), opened from the toolbar gear. A single centered modal limited to: Back up library (zip to Downloads, api.backup), Restore from a backup zip (window.himmy.pickZip + api.restore), Reveal library folder in Finder (window.himmy.revealData), and the data-dir path display (api.dataDir). Honest note that real-time phone sync isn't available.
surfaces.currentState: Complete but narrow — only backup/sync. SettingRow (App.tsx:1037) is a reusable labeled-control row.
gaps: This is the ONLY settings area and it is scoped to backup/sync. There is no general Preferences surface for: model/provider choice, news interests, Google account management (currently only reachable inside Mail/Calendar tabs), notification/reminder settings, or a RAG re-index control. A new 'Settings' could either expand this modal (add SettingRows) or become a full nav surface. The modal pattern + SettingRow component are the ready building blocks.

notableFindings: Entire UI is essentially two files: src/App.tsx (3,059 lines, all 8 surfaces + assistant) and src/Reader.tsx. New surfaces are added by (1) extending the Section union (App.tsx:25), (2) adding a NAV entry (App.tsx:26) or a Planner sub-tab (App.tsx:1415), and (3) adding a case in Content (App.tsx:218). No router, no per-surface files.

Two global event buses drive everything: window CustomEvents `himmy:nav` (navigate) and `himmy:ask` (open assistant), plus the in-module _refreshBus (App.tsx:38-73) that lets assistant tool-calls live-refresh open views. emitRefreshForTools (App.tsx:52) is the map from tool name → surface to refresh — a new write-tool-backed surface must be registered there to get live updates.

Best places for a NEW Schedules/Routines or Reminders surface: (a) a new sub-tab inside Planner (App.tsx:1408-1429) — mechanically the cheapest, sits beside Tasks/Calendar; or (b) a new top-level nav tab via NAV + Content + Section; or (c) a card on the Today home grid (App.tsx:340) for an at-a-glance view. Reminders specifically map naturally onto the existing Tasks model (which already has a free-text `due`) but would need a real date/recurrence picker — none exists today.

The backend already exposes scheduling-adjacent primitives the UI could build on: tasks (with `due`), calendar create/update/remove, and the assistant's tool-approval (HITL) machinery. There is NO frontend for himmy 'routines'/cron — the desktop app has no Routines/Schedules concept yet (consistent with the himmy-scheduling memory note that routines lack a UI).
Unwired capability: api.index(force) (RAG re-index, api.ts:223) has no UI trigger anywhere — a natural addition to a future Settings surface.

Settings is a single modal scoped only to Backup & Sync (App.tsx:970); Google account connect/disconnect lives inside the Mail/Calendar tabs (useGoogle hook App.tsx:1141), and news/research interests are edited inline in News and Library. A consolidated Preferences/Settings surface is an obvious gap and a clean home for: model display, interests, Google account, RAG re-index, and any new reminder/notification settings.

Reusable design building blocks for new UI: Card (App.tsx:2422), Placeholder (App.tsx:2445), Stub (App.tsx:1123, with an 'Ask Himmy' CTA), Step (App.tsx:567, an unused onboarding-checklist row), SettingRow (App.tsx:1037), and the Google connect/gate pattern (GoogleConnect App.tsx:1233). New surfaces should reuse these + the mac.* Tailwind tokens to match the language.

Electron model: single window, backend spawned from .venv/bin/python -m himmy_app.server (main.cjs:77), health-checked on :8131; preload exposes only narrow native capabilities. New UI that needs native access (file dialogs, external URLs) must add an ipcMain.handle in main.cjs + a method in preload.cjs (window.himmy).

synthesis.headlineRecommendation: Wire himmy's already-built Routines scheduler into scholar-desk's FastAPI app — this is the single highest-leverage move because the framework ships a production-hardened scheduling subsystem that the app currently uses NONE of, and it unlocks a whole class of "set it and forget it" recurring experiences (morning briefing, daily news digest, due-date reminders, periodic library enrichment) with very little new code. Concretely: start the in-process RoutineScheduler from a new FastAPI lifespan in create_app(), add a thin Routines CRUD on the existing custom server (delegating to himmy's get_routines_store()/get_scheduler(), NOT mounting the multi-tenant /v1 router), and surface a "Routines/Schedules" sub-tab in the Planner. Sequence the rest by impact-to-effort: (2) persist the RAG index with SqliteKnowledgeBackend so the ~2-3 min cold rebuild on every launch disappears (the framework docstring literally names this Daybook case); (3) deliver scheduled results as native macOS notifications + an in-app inbox; then memory auto-injection, thumbs feedback into self-learning, guardrail DLP redaction, and a research council. Order routines FIRST because every downstream automation (digest, reminders, enrichment) depends on the scheduler existing.

opportunities.title: Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler into the app + a Routines CRUD
opportunities.whatUserGets: The app can now run saved automations on a schedule while it's open: 'every morning at 7:00 summarize my new arXiv papers', 'every 6 hours check for new citations', 'on July 1 at 9am remind me to renew'. Until this exists, NOTHING in the app can fire on a timer — this is the foundation every other automation feature below sits on.
opportunities.howItWorks: Add a FastAPI lifespan to create_app() in server.py: on startup set HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH to the data dir (alongside the existing HIMMY_TASKS_PATH/HIMMY_CONVERSATIONS_PATH pins in config.py) so .himmy/routines.db lives under .scholar-desk and survives restarts, then call get_scheduler().start() and await get_scheduler().catch_up_on_launch(); on shutdown await get_scheduler().stop(). The app currently has NO lifespan (app=create_app() at server.py:826, plain FastAPI() at :178), so this is net-new. Add ~5 thin endpoints on the existing custom app — GET/POST /routines, PUT/DELETE /routines/{id}, POST /routines/{id}/run-now — each delegating to get_routines_store() + (for run-now) await get_scheduler().run_now(id); call get_scheduler().notify_change() after every CRUD write so an edited schedule re-plans immediately. Copy the start/notify/catch_up wiring from himmy's studio_routines.py router (its _wake_scheduler helper) but do NOT mount the /v1 router (it carries RBAC/multi-tenant machinery the single-user app doesn't use). Routines bind by agent_path to the existing agent/agent.yaml, so they reuse the exact agent brain the chat already runs.

opportunities.title: Persist the papers RAG index with SqliteKnowledgeBackend (kill the cold-start rebuild)
opportunities.whatUserGets: 'Ask my library' works instantly on launch instead of spinning for 2-3 minutes re-embedding every PDF. The knowledge base survives restarts; only genuinely new/changed papers get embedded thanks to content-hash dedup.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: KnowledgeBase backend=SqliteKnowledgeBackend(path) (the offline twin of pgvector; its docstring explicitly names this Daybook ~2-min re-embed problem) + local_embedders.build_embedder('auto') for genuinely semantic recall
opportunities.howItWorks: In papers_rag.py PapersIndex.__init__/build, the KnowledgeBase is built in-memory and rebuilt each launch (only extracted PDF text is cached in papers_cache.db). Change the KnowledgeBase construction to pass backend=SqliteKnowledgeBackend(<data_dir>/papers_index.db) so embeddings+chunks+fingerprint persist; content-addressed ingest then skips unchanged papers on the next launch. Also swap the DeterministicEmbedder for build_embedder('auto') so recall is real semantic similarity when fastembed/Ollama is present (embeddings extra is already in requirements.txt: himmy[...,embeddings]). KB_WORKSPACE_ID/KB_CLIENT_ID/KB_NAME are already set so resolve_kb keeps the durable KB stable by name across runs. Optionally wire api.index(force) (already in api.ts:223 but UNbound to any UI) to a 'Rebuild knowledge base' button.

opportunities.title: Scheduled morning briefing + daily news digest routine
opportunities.whatUserGets: A 'Daily Briefing' that runs unattended (e.g. weekdays 6:30 Kathmandu time): summarizes new papers in the library, top saved-news, today's calendar, and open/overdue tasks — ready and waiting when the user opens the app. The flagship recurring experience for a personal research workspace.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwSched: Routine + Schedule(kind='cron'/'daily', timezone=, missed='coalesce') firing through the same agent pipeline; plus the existing news.py feed + tasks store + calendar endpoints as the data the prompt pulls
opportunities.howItWorks: Seed a built-in 'Daily Briefing' Routine via get_routines_store().upsert() pointing at agent/agent.yaml with a prompt that asks the agent to use its existing tools (ask_papers, list_tasks, gmail_inbox, calendar_find) to assemble a briefing. Use Schedule(kind='cron', expr='30 6 * * 1-5', timezone='Asia/Kathmandu', missed='coalesce') so a briefing missed while the Mac slept fires exactly once on wake instead of skipping or storming backfills. This needs the [cron] extra — add 'cron' to the himmy[...] pin in requirements.txt (daily/every kinds work with zero extra deps if you skip cron grammar).
