Persist each briefing's result text into a new 'briefings' table (or reuse saved_news/notes) so the Today home grid can render the latest one in a card (Today() around App.tsx:340 using the shared Card component).
opportunities.dependsOn: Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler into the app + a Routines CRUD

opportunities.title: Native macOS notification + in-app inbox for routine results
opportunities.whatUserGets: When a scheduled briefing/digest/reminder finishes, the user gets a real macOS notification and a badge/inbox entry in-app — so automations are actually seen, not silently logged. Also surfaces 'routine needs approval' when an automation hits a gated tool, parking it safely instead of acting unattended.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwSched: result delivery + lifecycle notifications (_deliver/_notify; deliver kinds limited to none/telegram/email, so the desktop-notification channel is added by post-processing the routine result). Plus the framework's approval-gating which auto-parks gated tools.
opportunities.howItWorks: Built-in deliver kinds are only none/telegram/email, so for a native macOS toast scholar-desk post-processes the routine result itself: have the briefing/digest routines write their result into a new 'inbox' table, then expose GET /notifications. In Electron main.cjs add an ipcMain.handle('notify:show', ...) using electron's Notification (currently UNUSED — main.cjs only spawns/kills the backend) and a method in preload.cjs (window.himmy.notify). The renderer polls /notifications (mirroring the existing 5s/15s polls in App.tsx) and on a new entry calls window.himmy.notify(...) and shows an inbox badge in the toolbar (online/offline dot logic already computed but unused in the header — same spot). Approval-gated routine runs already park as awaiting_approval and emit an in-app notification deep-link via the framework, routing into the existing HITL approval UI (ApprovalCard, /ask/resume).
opportunities.dependsOn: Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler into the app + a Routines CRUD

opportunities.title: Real task due-dates + reminder routine (act on the dead `due` column)
opportunities.whatUserGets: Tasks get a real date picker and the app actually reminds the user when something is due or overdue — today the `due` column exists in the store and is returned to the UI but NOTHING reads or fires on it, and add_task only accepts a title.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwSched: a recurring Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=N or 'daily') that scans the tasks store for due/overdue items and emits a notification; fwKernels structured/constrained output (optional) to parse a natural-language due date into an ISO date
opportunities.howItWorks: Two parts. (a) Make due-dates settable: the add_task tool takes only `title` (agent_tools.py), and there's no /tasks edit endpoint — add a due param to the task-create path and a PUT /tasks/{id} reschedule endpoint, plus a date picker in TaskRow (App.tsx ~1819; isOverdue at App.tsx:559 is already a best-effort parse waiting for real data). (b) Seed a built-in 'Reminders' Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=1) whose prompt lists tasks via list_tasks and flags due/overdue ones, delivering via the notification channel from the inbox feature. Optionally use constrained decoding (output_json_schema + metadata={'constrained_decoding':true}) so 'tomorrow 3pm' parses to a guaranteed-valid ISO date instead of a fragile free-text string.
opportunities.dependsOn: Native macOS notification + in-app inbox for routine results

opportunities.title: Memory auto-injection so Himmy silently remembers across sessions
opportunities.whatUserGets: Himmy stops being a stateless palette and quietly recalls the user's projects, preferences, and prior conclusions every turn — without the user having to explicitly ask it to recall — and stops storing duplicate facts.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: MemoryContextAdapter (auto-injects recalled memories into the system prompt with a noise floor) + MemoryConsolidator (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP instead of blind append). The app already wires register_memory_pack (durable remember/recall) but uses neither of these.
opportunities.howItWorks: The memory pack is already registered in agent_tools.py against the durable .scholar-desk/memory.db (HIMMY_MEMORY_PATH set in config.py). Two additive steps: (1) construct a MemoryContextAdapter(memory, top_k=, tiers=) and register it on the ContextService used by the run pipeline (in cli.py's runtime build) so the agent sees relevant prior facts each turn with no explicit recall tool-call; set memory_subject to the per-user workspace. (2) Enable memory_consolidate in ToolkitConfig (the consolidate tool) so re-stating a fact updates rather than duplicates. Both are offline and fail-open.

opportunities.title: Thumbs up/down on answers feeding self-learning
opportunities.whatUserGets: A simple 👍/👎 on each assistant answer measurably shifts which tools Himmy prefers next time — flaky tools/connectors get deprioritized and the assistant adapts to this one user instead of staying static.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: OutcomeRecorder.record(score, source=USER_FEEDBACK) feeding the already-on self-learning loop (LearningService + ToolReputationProvider). agent.yaml already has self_learning: true (confirmed line 156) but no user-feedback signal is wired and outcome_weight isn't set.
opportunities.howItWorks: self_learning is already enabled, so LearningService + ToolReputationProvider + LearnedHintsContextAdapter are already mining TOOL_FAILED/TOOL_COMPLETED. The missing lever is the OUTCOME_SCORED user signal: set outcome_weight>0 in agent.yaml, add a POST /feedback {session_id, turn, score} endpoint that calls OutcomeRecorder.record(score, source=OutcomeSource.USER_FEEDBACK) against the same SqliteSessionStore/event log the app uses, and add a thumbs control to the chat Bubble (App.tsx ~2882). Because outcome_weight>0, those grades blend into per-tool reputation and reorder tool selection.

opportunities.title: Always-on background automations engine via spawned `himmy worker`
opportunities.whatUserGets: Scheduled briefings/digests/reminders keep firing even when the UI is closed or restarted (e.g. an overnight digest is ready at 7am whether or not the window was open), because timing no longer depends on the FastAPI process staying alive.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwSched: standalone `himmy worker` (brings up the durable substrate + RoutineScheduler with no API server) OR the launchd-driven OS-cron bridge `himmy routines run-now <id>` (durable single fire, flock-guarded against double-execution)
opportunities.howItWorks: Two options. (A) In Electron main.cjs spawn `.venv/bin/python -m himmy worker --store <data-dir>/runs.db` on app launch (next to the existing backend spawn at main.cjs:77) and kill it on before-quit (main.cjs:135). (B) For survival even when the app is fully quit, register a macOS launchd job that runs `himmy routines run-now <id>` (or calls himmy.api.routines.execute_routine(id)); the cross-process flock guarantees it never collides with the in-process scheduler. The in-process scheduler (first feature) already covers the common 'app is open' case, so this is the upgrade for the 'machine asleep / app closed' case — lower priority but the only way to be truly always-on.
opportunities.dependsOn: Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler into the app + a Routines CRUD

opportunities.title: Privacy guardrails: redact PII before prompts leave for the cloud model
opportunities.whatUserGets: Personal info is redacted before any text is sent to the cloud model (OpenRouter/Gemini), fetched web/PDF content is screened for prompt-injection, and the user sees a 'what was redacted' indicator — a real trust feature for a personal workspace whose backend forwards prompts off-device.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: build_guardrail_pipeline(['pii','injection']).inspect() on input/output + SqliteTokenVaultBackend (local, reversible DLP) + build_guardrail_pre_hook for tool args; suppresses_output_content() to know when to buffer a streamed turn
opportunities.howItWorks: Not used today. In server.py /ask and /ask/stream, run pipeline.inspect() on the user prompt before the run and on the model output after; since the app streams, check pipeline.suppresses_output_content() to buffer-and-guard before emitting tokens (the existing /ask/stream is already buffered-then-rechunked, so this fits the current shape). Register build_guardrail_pre_hook on the ToolRegistry (agent_tools.py) so outbound tool args (mail_send, calendar writes) are redacted. Use SqliteTokenVaultBackend(<data_dir>/vault.db) for reversible local tokenization. Surface flagged GuardrailTriggers as a small 'redacted N items' note in the chat UI.

opportunities.title: Periodic library enrichment + new-citation routine
opportunities.whatUserGets: The library quietly keeps itself tidy: papers missing metadata get auto-enriched, and the user is periodically alerted to new arXiv recommendations matching their interests — set-and-forget upkeep instead of manual per-paper enrich clicks.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwSched: recurring Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=6) running through the agent; reusing the existing library enrich endpoint and news recommendations surface
opportunities.howItWorks: Seed a built-in 'Library upkeep' Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=6) whose prompt scans library items lacking metadata and calls the existing enrich path (library.py POST /library/{id}/enrich), and a prompt leg that pulls news recommendations (already in news.py / api.news.recommendations) and flags new arXiv candidates. Deliver via the notification/inbox channel. Note: enrich depends on OPENROUTER_API_KEY (silently no-ops without it), so the routine should skip gracefully when absent. Optionally add a save-to-library shortcut endpoint (the recommendations surface currently has none and no dedup against existing items).
opportunities.dependsOn: Native macOS notification + in-app inbox for routine results

opportunities.title: Deep Research council (multi-agent gather→draft→critique→revise)
opportunities.whatUserGets: A meaningfully more capable 'Deep Research' mode that fans a question across specialist sub-agents and shows the intermediate reasoning, plus long reports that survive an app restart and can be resumed.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: MultiAgentOrchestrator (handoff/delegate teams) + StateGraph (durable interrupt/resume on the already-wired SqliteCheckpointStore). The app already uses PlannerOrchestrator + reflect() in research.py — this is the next rung.
opportunities.howItWorks: research.py already runs PlannerOrchestrator + reflect via _build_runtime (deterministic library leg + best-effort web leg). Upgrade to a MultiAgentOrchestrator AgentTeam: a 'searcher' delegate (ask_papers + web search), an 'analyst' that synthesizes, and a 'critic' worker — built with the same _build_runtime. For long multi-stage reports, model the flow as a StateGraph with NodeInterrupt so a partial report persists across restarts (SqliteCheckpointStore is already wired). Note the web leg is keyless DuckDuckGo and usually empty without HIMMY_SEARCH_API_KEY (tavily/brave), so this pairs well with surfacing a search-key setting. Persist briefs (currently saved nowhere) so past research is browsable.

opportunities.title: Activity & Provenance timeline (EntityRecord/RunEvent audit spine)
opportunities.whatUserGets: A trustworthy 'what Himmy did' timeline: tools called, what it read, what it remembered, why it chose an answer, with per-answer source links — transparency a power-user research desktop benefits from.
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: EntityRecord audit spine + RunEvent stream (TOOL_CALLED/COMPLETED/FAILED, OUTCOME_SCORED, MEMORY_*, LEARNING_APPLIED) read off the same storage.db the app already has; EntityLinks to relate a note→paper→run
opportunities.howItWorks: storage.db (the durable entity/audit spine) already exists in .scholar-desk and SqliteSessionStore is wired. Pass an EntityRegistry into MemoryService so memory writes project onto the spine, and read the RunEvent stream to build a GET /activity feed. Render it as a new read-only surface — cheapest as a Today card or a Planner sub-tab (the Section union + NAV + Content switch pattern in App.tsx:25/26/218, or the Planner sub-tab array at App.tsx:1415). EntityLinks can relate a derived note to its source paper and the run that produced it. Largely an observability/read surface over data the app is already generating once self_learning/memory events flow.

opportunities.title: Regression benchmark suite for the research agent (BFCL-grade)
opportunities.whatUserGets: Confidence that any prompt/model/tool change actually improves the assistant before it ships to the user, plus a principled way to pick the smallest local model that still answers research questions well (matters for an offline-first desktop app).
opportunities.frameworkCapabilityUsed: fwKernels: BenchmarkRunner + graders (arg-level/trajectory) + judge + stats (`himmy bench`), or AgentEvalHarness.evaluate_agent
opportunities.howItWorks: Not a user-facing feature — an internal eng gate. Author a small in-repo YAML suite of representative tasks (ask_papers retrieval, citation formatting, calendar/gmail tool selection, 'should NOT call a tool' irrelevance cases) and run BenchmarkRunner with an inference_factory pointing at the app's configured model. Use it as a check before changing agent.yaml/prompts/tools and to compare candidate local models. Low user impact but high developer-velocity value and cheap to stand up.

missedCapabilities: Multi-surface backup coverage (verified at library.py:558-568): backup() zips ONLY library.db + library_files/. The appBackend map flags that conversations.db, memory.db, tasks.db, usage.json (and a future routines.db) are NOT backed up, and restore is destructive. No opportunity proposes a 'whole-workspace backup' (and crucially, once Routines/memory-consolidation/self-learning add valuable durable state, leaving them out of backup becomes a real data-loss risk). This is a cheap, high-trust win the synthesis missed.

ConnectorSpec YAML / OutboundToolConnector SDK migration as its OWN opportunity (fwKernels 'CONNECTORS framework', maturity=built). The map explicitly notes scholar-desk hand-rolls its connectors with safe_register_local_tool and bypasses the SDK, losing SSRF guarding, secrets-layer credential resolution, rate limiting, idempotency, and built-in requires_approval HITL. The map also calls out that a ConnectorService catalog could back a 'Connections' settings UI. The proposals reference connectors only obliquely inside the guardrails item; there is no opportunity to (a) declare arXiv/Crossref/Semantic-Scholar/Unpaywall (already used in library.py) as ConnectorSpec YAML, or (b) surface a managed/testable Connections panel — both directly enabled by a built capability.

Bi-temporal recall + typed memory GRAPH + EntityLinks as a USER-FACING feature (fwKernels memory: as_of point-in-time recall, MemoryLink graph, traverse_graph, recall(max_hops=...)). The map's appOpportunity literally describes a 'what I knew as of <date>' time-travel view and a related-facts panel (paper->note->task). The 'memory auto-injection' opportunity only adopts MemoryContextAdapter + consolidation; it omits the temporal/graph layer that turns linked notes/papers/tasks into a navigable knowledge graph — a distinctive research-desk feature.

Structured/constrained decoding (CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY + output_json_schema) as a STANDALONE high-value feature for the Library (fwKernels structured output, maturity=built). The map's appOpportunity is auto-extracting title/authors/venue/DOI from a dropped PDF into a fillable form and guaranteed-parse citation objects. This maps onto a real existing gap: library enrich currently has the AI read page 1 and parse prose. Constrained decoding appears only as an optional aside inside the task-due-date item, not as its own metadata-extraction opportunity where it has the clearest payoff.

Full-text search inside the Library tab (appBackend Library gap: list() filters only title/author/venue/tags; full-text is ONLY via RAG). With SqliteKnowledgeBackend persistence landing (proposal #2), a 'search everything I've read' surface over the durable KB becomes nearly free, but no opportunity proposes exposing in-library full-text/semantic search to the user as a UI surface distinct from the chat agent.

Surfacing highlights/annotations to RAG/agent (appBackend Highlights gap: 'Highlights not surfaced to the RAG/agent (only the item-level note is)'). The user's own margin notes are arguably the highest-signal text in the library yet are invisible to ask_papers. Feeding highlights into the (now-durable) KB is a small, high-value change no opportunity covers.
overreach: 'Privacy guardrails: redact PII before prompts leave for the cloud model' is largely ALREADY BUILT and wired, not a net-new feature. Verified at from_spec.py:281-298: build_runtime_for_spec already constructs an input_guardrail pipeline from spec.guardrails, an output_guardrail (grounding + spec guardrails), AND pre/post tool-execution hooks (build_guardrail_pre_hook/post_hook at lines 510-511). agent.yaml already declares 'guardrails: [pii, injection]'. So PII redaction on input, injection screening, and tool-arg redaction are ACTIVE TODAY. The genuinely-unwired delta is only: (a) the reversible DLP TokenVault (SqliteTokenVaultBackend) and (b) a UI 'what was redacted' indicator surfacing GuardrailTriggers. The opportunity overstates the work by presenting the whole pipeline as unwired; it should be rescoped to just the DLP vault + the redaction-indicator UI.

'Persist the RAG index... swap the DeterministicEmbedder for build_embedder("auto")' is partly inaccurate. Verified at papers_rag.py:177/184: the app already calls ToolkitConfig.from_env().build_embedder_and_dim() (not a hardcoded DeterministicEmbedder), and the himmy[...,embeddings] extra ships fastembed, so recall is very likely ALREADY semantic. The real, accurate change is solely backend=None -> SqliteKnowledgeBackend(path) for persistence. The embedder-swap framing risks wasted effort or a regression; the opportunity's headline win (kill the cold rebuild) is correct, but the embedder claim should be dropped or verified-first.

'Thumbs up/down feeding self-learning' overstates novelty in its rationale but is otherwise sound. self_learning: true is confirmed in agent.yaml and the learning loop (LearningService/ToolReputationProvider/LearnedHintsContextAdapter) is already mining TOOL_FAILED/TOOL_COMPLETED. The new lever (OUTCOME_SCORED via OutcomeRecorder + outcome_weight>0 + a thumbs control) is genuinely unwired, so the opportunity is valid — but it should be clear that the self-learning ENGINE is already on; this only adds the user-feedback signal.

Feature #1's integration claim that 'the dispatcher/durable-queue is only needed for the multi-tenant agent_id path' is an oversimplification, though it lands on the right answer. Verified at routines.py:1140-1210: the single-user agent_path path executes through studio_service.stream_agent_run + resolve_canonical_storage() (NOT the durable RunDispatcher), so the in-process scheduler can indeed skip the worker/dispatcher. But it DOES depend on resolve_canonical_storage() returning a usable canonical store and on studio_service being importable/functional inside scholar-desk's custom (non-BFF) app. This is an integration risk to verify, not the no-dependency drop-in the wording implies.

'Always-on background automations engine via spawned himmy worker' is marked dependsOn the in-process scheduler, but per the map the worker is an ALTERNATIVE firing mechanism, not a downstream consumer — it brings up its OWN RoutineScheduler. Running BOTH the in-process scheduler (in the FastAPI app) and a spawned worker simultaneously means two schedulers; the map notes the flock guard prevents double-fire, but the proposal should explicitly say worker is an either/or upgrade path (worker OR in-process loop), not an additive layer on top of feature #1.

Several opportunities silently depend on himmy[cron] which is NOT in requirements.txt today (verified: 'himmy[toolkit,api,openai,embeddings]', no cron). The morning-briefing and reminder routines use cron expressions ('30 6 * * 1-5'). Per fwSched, cron strings are accepted at create-time but only error at FIRE time without the cronsim extra. So a cron routine seeded without adding 'cron' to the extras will silently never fire correctly — a latent footgun the briefing/reminder opportunities under-emphasize (the briefing item mentions it; the reminders item leans on kind='every' which is safe, but any cron grammar in the UI needs the extra).
sequencing: FOUNDATION (build first): 'Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler + Routines CRUD' is correctly identified as the root dependency. Confirmed there is NO lifespan today (server.py:175 create_app, :826 app=create_app(), no lifespan) and zero scheduling code anywhere in src/himmy_app or desktop/electron. Nothing else in the automation chain (briefing, digest, reminders, library upkeep) can fire until this exists. Build it first.

BEFORE seeding any cron-based routine: add 'cron' to the himmy extras in requirements.txt (currently himmy[toolkit,api,openai,embeddings]). Cron strings parse at create-time but only fail at fire-time without cronsim, so a cron routine would silently never fire. Also set HIMMY_TZ=Asia/Kathmandu (Nepal user) so 'daily 07:00'/'30 6 * * 1-5' mean local time, and rely on missed='coalesce' for laptop-sleep safety. This is a prerequisite for the morning-briefing item, not a footnote.

BEFORE wiring routines.db: pin HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH into the .scholar-desk data dir (mirroring the existing HIMMY_TASKS_PATH/HIMMY_CONVERSATIONS_PATH pins in config.py). get_routines_store() resolves .himmy/routines.db relative to cwd, and the Electron-spawned backend's cwd is not guaranteed stable, so without this pin routines could be created in one location and read from another across launches — silently 'losing' saved automations. This config pin must land in the same change as feature #1.

Notification/inbox (feature #3) must precede the user-visible automation payoffs (reminders feature #5, library-upkeep feature #11) — the proposal sequences these correctly. Built-in deliver kinds are only none/telegram/email (fwSched delivery, maturity=partial), so a desktop user sees NOTHING unless the result is post-processed into an in-app inbox + a native macOS Notification (electron Notification is confirmed unused in main.cjs). Without #3, scheduled briefings run but are invisible — building reminders before notifications produces silent automations.

VERIFY-FIRST gate before committing to feature #1's effort estimate: confirm resolve_canonical_storage() returns a usable store and studio_service.stream_agent_run runs end-to-end inside scholar-desk's custom (non-BFF) FastAPI app with a routine bound by agent_path to agent/agent.yaml. The agent_path path routes through the Studio run pipeline (routines.py:1154-1205), and load_studio_spec honors tools_module so the app's custom tools (ask_papers, calendar_*) WILL be available — but this whole chain has never run in this app, so a thin spike (create one 'every 5 min' test routine, confirm it fires through the real agent and lands its result) should precede the full CRUD + UI build.

Persist-RAG (feature #2) and memory auto-injection (feature #6) are independent of the scheduler and can be built in parallel with / before feature #1. Both are pure-additive, fail-open, touch no scheduling code, and deliver immediate user value (instant 'ask my library', cross-session memory). Front-load #2 especially — it's effort=S, removes a known 2-3 min launch stall, and has zero dependency on the routines work.

Backup-coverage expansion should be sequenced EARLY relative to the new durable stores, not deferred. Once routines.db, richer memory (consolidation), and self-learning reputation accumulate value, the existing library-only backup (verified library.py:558-568) leaves them exposed and restore is destructive. Widen backup to the full .scholar-desk set before/as those stores fill, so the first scheduled-automation user isn't a data-loss case.

additionalOpportunities.title: Whole-workspace backup + restore (not just library.db + PDFs)
additionalOpportunities.whatUserGets: A real 'back up everything' that protects conversations, durable memory, tasks, usage history, and (once it exists) saved routines — not just papers. Today backup() zips only library.db + library_files/ (verified library.py:558-568), and restore is destructive, so a sync mishap silently loses chat history, remembered facts, and the task board.
additionalOpportunities.why: appBackend Backup/Restore + Persisted-data gaps explicitly list conversations.db, memory.db, tasks.db, usage.json as unprotected; this becomes higher-stakes the moment Routines/self-learning/memory-consolidation add valuable state. Pure app-side change, no framework dependency, high trust payoff.

additionalOpportunities.title: Declare arXiv/Crossref/Unpaywall/Semantic-Scholar as ConnectorSpec YAML + a managed 'Connections' settings panel
additionalOpportunities.whatUserGets: A trustworthy Connections settings surface (enable/test/status per integration) where paper-API and side-effecting tools get SSRF guarding, secrets-layer credentials, rate limiting, idempotency, and requires_approval HITL for free — instead of the current ad-hoc hand-rolled connectors.
additionalOpportunities.why: fwKernels CONNECTORS framework (maturity=built) explicitly notes scholar-desk hand-rolls connectors via safe_register_local_tool, bypassing the SDK, and that ConnectorService could back a Settings 'Connections' UI. The REST integrations are already in library.py — re-expressing them as ConnectorSpec YAML is the framework's lowest-effort path and was entirely absent from the proposals.

additionalOpportunities.title: Constrained-decoding metadata extraction for the Library (drop-a-PDF -> guaranteed-valid fields)
additionalOpportunities.whatUserGets: Dropping a PDF auto-fills a fillable title/authors/venue/year/DOI form that always parses, and citation objects that never break — replacing the current 'AI reads page 1 as prose -> Crossref' enrich path with schema-constrained output.
additionalOpportunities.why: fwKernels structured output (maturity=built; CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY + output_json_schema, fail-open) has its clearest payoff exactly on the Library enrich/citation surface, an existing gap. The proposals only mention constrained decoding as an optional aside under task due-dates, missing the highest-value application.

additionalOpportunities.title: Time-travel + related-facts knowledge graph over memory (as_of recall + EntityLinks panel)
additionalOpportunities.whatUserGets: A 'what did I believe as of <date>' view and a related-items panel that surfaces linked notes/papers/tasks for whatever the user is viewing — turning memory from a flat fact list into a navigable graph.
additionalOpportunities.why: fwKernels memory (built) ships bi-temporal recall (as_of/active_only), a typed MemoryLink graph, traverse_graph, and recall(max_hops=). The 'memory auto-injection' opportunity adopts only the adapter+consolidation and leaves these distinctive research-desk features on the table; the map's own appOpportunity describes exactly this.

additionalOpportunities.title: Surface PDF highlights/annotations into the durable RAG index
additionalOpportunities.whatUserGets: ask_papers and Deep Research can cite the user's OWN margin highlights and notes, not just the raw paper text — the highest-signal content in the library becomes searchable and answerable.
additionalOpportunities.why: appBackend Highlights gap states highlights are NOT surfaced to RAG (only the item-level note is). With SqliteKnowledgeBackend persistence (proposal #2) landing, ingesting highlights as additional documents is a small change with outsized retrieval-quality impact, and no proposal covers it.

finalRoadmap.headline: Stand up himmy's already-built Routines scheduler inside the app FIRST — it's the one thing that lets anything fire on a timer (briefings, digests, reminders, upkeep), and the framework ships the whole subsystem so it's mostly wiring, not new engine. Sequence everything else by impact-to-effort, correcting the proposal where the work is already done: the privacy guardrail pipeline is ALREADY live (only the reversible DLP vault + a 'what was redacted' badge remain), and the RAG embedder is ALREADY semantic (the real win is just persisting the index). Add the capabilities the proposal missed: whole-workspace backup (today only papers are backed up — a real data-loss risk once routines/memory accumulate), proper connectors, drop-a-PDF metadata extraction, and feeding the user's own highlights into search.
finalRoadmap.buildFirst: Wire himmy's in-process RoutineScheduler into the FastAPI app via a new lifespan in create_app() (server.py:175 — confirmed NO lifespan exists today; app=create_app() at server.py:826 is a plain FastAPI), pin HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH into the .scholar-desk data dir in config.py (mirroring the existing HIMMY_STORE_PATH/HIMMY_MEMORY_PATH/HIMMY_TASKS_PATH/HIMMY_CONVERSATIONS_PATH setdefaults at config.py:73-84), add 'cron' + set HIMMY_TZ=Asia/Kathmandu, and add a thin Routines CRUD delegating to get_routines_store()/get_scheduler() (NOT the multi-tenant /v1 router). Nothing else in the automation chain can fire until this exists. PRECEDE it with a one-routine spike (an 'every 5 min' test) to verify resolve_canonical_storage() + studio_service.stream_agent_run actually run end-to-end through agent/agent.yaml inside this custom non-BFF app — that chain has never run here.
recommendedFirstSprint: VERIFY-FIRST SPIKE: seed ONE Routine bound by agent_path to agent/agent.yaml on a kind='every', minutes=5 schedule and confirm it fires through the real agent (resolve_canonical_storage + studio_service.stream_agent_run) and lands a result — this de-risks the whole effort estimate before building UI.

FOUNDATION: add the FastAPI lifespan to create_app() (get_scheduler().start() + await catch_up_on_launch() on startup, await stop() on shutdown), pin HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH into .scholar-desk in config.py, add 'cron' to the himmy extras in requirements.txt and set HIMMY_TZ=Asia/Kathmandu — all in ONE change. Then add ~5 endpoints (GET/POST /routines, PUT/DELETE /routines/{id}, POST /routines/{id}/run-now) calling notify_change() after every write, plus a Routines sub-tab in the Planner.

PARALLEL QUICK WIN (no scheduler dependency, effort=S): persist the RAG index by passing backend=SqliteKnowledgeBackend(<data_dir>/papers_index.db) in papers_rag.py:183-184 (change ONLY backend=None — do NOT touch the embedder; it already uses build_embedder_and_dim() so recall is already semantic). Kills the 2-3 min cold-start re-embed.

Widen backup() in library.py:558-568 to a whole-workspace zip (conversations.db, memory.db, tasks.db, usage.json, storage.db, and the new routines.db) and make restore non-destructive — do this BEFORE routines/memory state accumulates, so the first automation user isn't a data-loss case.

Build the native macOS notification + in-app inbox (electron Notification is confirmed unused in main.cjs) so the FIRST scheduled briefing is actually seen, not silently logged — this must precede any user-facing automation payoff.

schedulingFeatures.title: Wire the in-process RoutineScheduler into the app + a Routines CRUD (THE FOUNDATION)
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: The app can run saved automations on a schedule while it's open: 'every weekday 6:30am summarize my new arXiv papers', 'every 6 hours check for new citations', 'July 1 at 9am remind me to renew'. Until this exists, NOTHING in the app can fire on a timer — every other automation below sits on this.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy RoutineScheduler (get_scheduler().start()/stop()/notify_change()/catch_up_on_launch()/run_now) + RoutinesStore (get_routines_store().upsert/list/get/delete) + Routine/Schedule models, all already production-hardened in the framework
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: Add a FastAPI lifespan to create_app() at server.py:175 (confirmed NO lifespan today; app=create_app() at :826 is plain FastAPI()). On startup: get_scheduler().start() + await catch_up_on_launch(); on shutdown: await stop(). Pin HIMMY_ROUTINES_PATH into .scholar-desk in config.py next to the existing setdefaults at config.py:73-84 (get_routines_store() resolves .himmy/routines.db relative to cwd, and the Electron-spawned backend's cwd is not guaranteed stable, so without this pin saved automations silently 'disappear' across launches). Add ~5 thin endpoints delegating to get_routines_store() and (for run-now) await get_scheduler().run_now(id); call notify_change() after every CRUD write. Copy the start/notify/catch_up wiring from himmy's studio_routines.py _wake_scheduler helper but do NOT mount /v1 (RBAC/multi-tenant machinery the single-user app doesn't use). INTEGRATION RISK to verify first (routines.py:1140-1210): the single-user agent_path path runs through studio_service.stream_agent_run + resolve_canonical_storage(), NOT the durable RunDispatcher, so it can skip the worker — but that chain has never run inside scholar-desk's custom non-BFF app, so spike one test routine first. load_studio_spec honors tools_module so the app's custom tools (ask_papers, calendar_*) WILL be available.

schedulingFeatures.title: Add the 'cron' extra + Kathmandu timezone (PREREQUISITE, not a footnote)
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: Schedules expressed as real calendar rules ('weekdays at 6:30am', 'the 1st of every month') actually fire — and they fire at Nepal local time, and a job missed while the Mac slept fires exactly once on wake instead of being skipped or backfilled in a storm.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy Schedule(kind='cron', expr=..., timezone=, missed='coalesce'); the cronsim extra is what makes cron strings fire (they parse at create-time but only ERROR at fire-time without it — a silent footgun)
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: requirements.txt today is himmy[toolkit,api,openai,embeddings] — NO cron (confirmed). Add 'cron' to the extras. Set HIMMY_TZ=Asia/Kathmandu (the user is in Nepal) so '30 6 * * 1-5' and 'daily 07:00' mean local time. Rely on missed='coalesce' for laptop-sleep safety. This must land in the SAME change as the foundation, because the briefing/reminder routines below would otherwise be seeded with cron strings that never fire. (kind='every'/'daily' work with zero extra deps, so the reminders routine is safe even without this — but any cron grammar exposed in the UI needs the extra.)

schedulingFeatures.title: Native macOS notification + in-app inbox for routine results
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: When a scheduled briefing/digest/reminder finishes, the user gets a real macOS notification and an inbox badge in-app — so automations are actually SEEN, not silently logged. Build this BEFORE reminders/upkeep, or those run invisibly. Also surfaces 'routine needs approval' when an automation hits a gated tool, parking it safely instead of acting unattended.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy routine result delivery + lifecycle notifications; built-in deliver kinds are only none/telegram/email (delivery maturity=partial) so the desktop channel is added by post-processing the result. Plus the framework's approval-gating which auto-parks gated tools as awaiting_approval.
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: Routines write their result into a new 'inbox' table; expose GET /notifications. In main.cjs add ipcMain.handle('notify:show', ...) using electron's Notification (confirmed UNUSED today — main.cjs only spawns/kills the backend) + a preload.cjs method (window.himmy.notify). The renderer polls /notifications (mirroring the existing 5s/15s polls in App.tsx) and on a new entry calls window.himmy.notify(...) and shows a toolbar badge. Approval-gated runs park as awaiting_approval and route into the existing HITL UI (ApprovalCard, /ask/resume).

schedulingFeatures.title: Scheduled morning briefing + daily news digest routine (the flagship experience)
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: A 'Daily Briefing' that runs unattended (weekdays 6:30 Kathmandu time): new papers in the library, top saved news, today's calendar, open/overdue tasks — ready and waiting when the user opens the app. The signature recurring experience for a personal research workspace.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy Routine + Schedule(kind='cron', timezone=, missed='coalesce') firing through the same agent/agent.yaml pipeline the chat already uses; the existing news.py feed + tasks store + calendar endpoints as the data the prompt pulls
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: Seed a built-in 'Daily Briefing' Routine via get_routines_store().upsert() pointing at agent/agent.yaml with a prompt that uses existing tools (ask_papers, list_tasks, gmail_inbox, calendar_find). Use Schedule(kind='cron', expr='30 6 * * 1-5', timezone='Asia/Kathmandu', missed='coalesce'). REQUIRES the 'cron' extra (above). Persist each result into the inbox table so the Today home grid renders the latest in a Card (Today() around App.tsx:340).

schedulingFeatures.title: Real task due-dates + reminder routine (act on the dead `due` column)
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: Tasks get a real date picker and the app actually reminds the user when something is due or overdue — today the `due` column exists in the store and is returned to the UI but NOTHING reads or fires on it, and add_task only accepts a title.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy recurring Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=1 — SAFE without the cron extra) that scans the tasks store and emits a notification; optionally constrained decoding to parse 'tomorrow 3pm' into a guaranteed-valid ISO date
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: (a) add_task takes only `title` (agent_tools.py) and there's no /tasks edit endpoint — add a due param to the create path and a PUT /tasks/{id} reschedule endpoint, plus a date picker in TaskRow (App.tsx ~1819; isOverdue at App.tsx:559 already best-effort parses, waiting for real data). (b) Seed a 'Reminders' Routine (kind='every', hours=1) that lists tasks and flags due/overdue ones, delivering via the inbox/notification channel. Use kind='every' (NOT cron) so it fires with zero extra deps.

schedulingFeatures.title: Periodic library enrichment + new-citation routine
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: The library quietly keeps itself tidy: papers missing metadata get auto-enriched and the user is periodically alerted to new arXiv recommendations matching their interests — set-and-forget upkeep instead of manual per-paper enrich clicks.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy recurring Routine (Schedule kind='every', hours=6) running through the agent; reuses the existing library enrich endpoint and news recommendations surface
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: Seed a 'Library upkeep' Routine (kind='every', hours=6) that scans library items lacking metadata and calls the existing enrich path (library.py POST /library/{id}/enrich), plus a leg pulling news recommendations (news.py / api.news.recommendations) to flag new arXiv candidates. Deliver via the inbox channel. enrich depends on OPENROUTER_API_KEY (silently no-ops without it), so the routine should skip gracefully when absent. Optionally add a save-to-library shortcut endpoint (recommendations currently have none and no dedup against existing items).

schedulingFeatures.title: Always-on background worker (EITHER/OR upgrade, not an additive layer)
schedulingFeatures.whatUserGets: Scheduled briefings/digests/reminders keep firing even when the app window is closed (e.g. an overnight digest is ready at 7am whether or not the window was open), because timing no longer depends on the FastAPI process staying alive.
schedulingFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy standalone `himmy worker` (brings up its OWN durable substrate + RoutineScheduler, no API server) OR a launchd OS-cron bridge `himmy routines run-now <id>` (flock-guarded against double-fire)
schedulingFeatures.howItWorks: IMPORTANT — this is an ALTERNATIVE firing mechanism, not a consumer of the in-process scheduler: the worker brings up its OWN RoutineScheduler, so it's worker OR the in-process loop, not both stacked. Option A: spawn `.venv/bin/python -m himmy worker --store <data-dir>/runs.db` in main.cjs next to the existing backend spawn (main.cjs:77), kill on before-quit (main.cjs:135). Option B (survives full quit): a macOS launchd job running `himmy routines run-now <id>`; the cross-process flock prevents collision. The in-process scheduler (foundation) already covers 'app is open', so this is the 'machine asleep / app closed' upgrade — lower priority.

otherFeatures.title: Persist the papers RAG index with SqliteKnowledgeBackend (kill the cold-start rebuild)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: 'Ask my library' works instantly on launch instead of spinning 2-3 minutes re-embedding every PDF. The knowledge base survives restarts; only genuinely new/changed papers get embedded thanks to content-hash dedup.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy KnowledgeBase backend=SqliteKnowledgeBackend(path) — the offline twin of pgvector; its docstring explicitly names this Daybook ~2-min re-embed problem
otherFeatures.howItWorks: CORRECTION to the proposal: papers_rag.py:177/183-184 ALREADY uses ToolkitConfig.from_env().build_embedder_and_dim() (NOT a hardcoded DeterministicEmbedder), and himmy[...,embeddings] ships fastembed, so recall is already semantic — do NOT swap the embedder (risk of regression for no gain). The ONLY accurate change is backend=None -> SqliteKnowledgeBackend(<data_dir>/papers_index.db) so embeddings+chunks+fingerprint persist and content-addressed ingest skips unchanged papers next launch. KB_WORKSPACE_ID/KB_CLIENT_ID/KB_NAME already keep the KB stable by name. Optionally bind api.index(force) (already in api.ts:223 but UNwired) to a 'Rebuild knowledge base' button.

otherFeatures.title: Whole-workspace backup + restore (NOT just library.db + PDFs) — MISSED by the proposal
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A real 'back up everything' that protects conversations, durable memory, tasks, usage history, and saved routines — not just papers. Today backup() zips ONLY library.db + library_files/ (verified library.py:558-568) and restore is destructive, so a sync mishap silently loses chat history, remembered facts, and the task board.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: None — pure app-side change, no framework dependency, high-trust payoff
otherFeatures.howItWorks: Widen backup() (library.py:565-568 currently writes only library.db + library_files/*) to also include conversations.db, memory.db, tasks.db, usage.json, storage.db, and the new routines.db. Make restore() non-destructive (back up current state before overwrite). SEQUENCE THIS EARLY — the moment Routines/self-learning/memory-consolidation start accumulating valuable durable state, the library-only backup becomes a real data-loss risk; the first scheduled-automation user should not be a data-loss case.

otherFeatures.title: Memory auto-injection + consolidation (silent cross-session recall)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: Himmy stops being a stateless palette and quietly recalls the user's projects, preferences, and prior conclusions every turn — without being asked to recall — and stops storing duplicate facts.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy MemoryContextAdapter (auto-injects recalled memories with a noise floor) + MemoryConsolidator (ADD/UPDATE/DELETE/NOOP instead of blind append). The app already wires register_memory_pack but uses neither.
otherFeatures.howItWorks: The memory pack is already registered in agent_tools.py against .scholar-desk/memory.db (HIMMY_MEMORY_PATH set in config.py). (1) construct MemoryContextAdapter(memory, top_k=, tiers=) and register it on the ContextService in cli.py's runtime build so the agent sees relevant prior facts each turn with no explicit recall call; set memory_subject per-workspace. (2) Enable memory_consolidate in ToolkitConfig so re-stating a fact updates rather than duplicates. Both offline and fail-open.

otherFeatures.title: Constrained-decoding metadata extraction for the Library (drop-a-PDF -> guaranteed-valid fields) — MISSED by the proposal
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: Dropping a PDF auto-fills a fillable title/authors/venue/year/DOI form that always parses, and citation objects that never break — replacing the current 'AI reads page 1 as prose' enrich path with schema-constrained output.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy structured output (CONSTRAINED_DECODING_KEY + output_json_schema, fail-open, maturity=built)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: library enrich currently has the model read page 1 and parse prose. Add output_json_schema + metadata={'constrained_decoding':true} to the enrich call so title/authors/venue/year/DOI come back as a guaranteed-valid object instead of fragile free text. This is the highest-value application of constrained decoding (the proposal buried it as an aside under task due-dates). Pairs with a fillable form in the library detail UI.

otherFeatures.title: Surface PDF highlights/annotations into the durable RAG index — MISSED by the proposal
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: ask_papers and Deep Research can cite the user's OWN margin highlights and notes — the highest-signal text in the library — not just raw paper text.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy KnowledgeBase ingest (on the now-durable SqliteKnowledgeBackend)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: Today highlights are NOT surfaced to RAG (only the item-level note is). Once the durable backend lands, ingest each highlight/annotation as an additional document (tagged to its paper) in papers_rag.py. Small change, outsized retrieval-quality impact. Depends on the RAG-persistence feature.

otherFeatures.title: Thumbs up/down on answers feeding self-learning
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A simple thumbs up/down on each assistant answer measurably shifts which tools Himmy prefers next time — flaky tools/connectors get deprioritized and the assistant adapts to this one user.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy OutcomeRecorder.record(score, source=USER_FEEDBACK) feeding the already-ON self-learning loop (LearningService + ToolReputationProvider)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: CLARIFICATION: the self-learning ENGINE is already on (self_learning: true confirmed at agent.yaml:156; LearningService/ToolReputationProvider/LearnedHintsContextAdapter already mine TOOL_FAILED/TOOL_COMPLETED). The genuinely-unwired delta is only the OUTCOME_SCORED user signal: set outcome_weight>0 in agent.yaml, add POST /feedback {session_id,turn,score} calling OutcomeRecorder.record(score, source=OutcomeSource.USER_FEEDBACK) against the same SqliteSessionStore the app uses, and add a thumbs control to the chat Bubble (App.tsx ~2882).

otherFeatures.title: Reversible DLP vault + 'what was redacted' badge (RESCOPED — guardrails are ALREADY live)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: The user sees a small 'redacted N items' indicator and can recover the original text — peace of mind that personal info handling is visible and reversible.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy SqliteTokenVaultBackend (local, reversible DLP) + surfacing GuardrailTriggers
otherFeatures.howItWorks: MAJOR CORRECTION to the proposal: PII redaction, injection screening, AND tool-arg redaction are ALREADY ACTIVE TODAY. Verified at from_spec.py:281-298 — build_runtime_for_spec already constructs input/output guardrail pipelines from spec.guardrails plus pre/post tool-execution hooks (build_guardrail_pre_hook/post_hook at lines 510-511), and agent.yaml:142-144 declares guardrails: [pii, injection]. The proposal's 'redact before prompts leave for the cloud' work is DONE. The genuinely-unwired delta is ONLY: (a) the reversible DLP TokenVault — use SqliteTokenVaultBackend(<data_dir>/vault.db); and (b) a UI 'what was redacted' indicator surfacing GuardrailTriggers in the chat. Much smaller than the proposal implied.

otherFeatures.title: Declare paper APIs as ConnectorSpec YAML + a managed 'Connections' settings panel — MISSED by the proposal
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A trustworthy Connections settings surface (enable/test/status per integration) where paper-API and side-effecting tools get SSRF guarding, secrets-layer credentials, rate limiting, idempotency, and requires_approval HITL for free — instead of today's ad-hoc hand-rolled connectors.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy CONNECTORS framework (ConnectorSpec YAML / OutboundToolConnector SDK + ConnectorService catalog, maturity=built)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: scholar-desk hand-rolls connectors via safe_register_local_tool, bypassing the SDK and losing SSRF/secrets/rate-limit/idempotency/HITL. The arXiv/Crossref/Semantic-Scholar/Unpaywall REST integrations already in library.py can be re-expressed as ConnectorSpec YAML, and ConnectorService can back a 'Connections' Settings UI. Lowest-effort framework-native path; entirely absent from the proposals.

otherFeatures.title: Deep Research council (multi-agent gather->draft->critique->revise)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A more capable 'Deep Research' mode that fans a question across specialist sub-agents and shows intermediate reasoning, plus long reports that survive an app restart and can be resumed.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy MultiAgentOrchestrator (handoff/delegate teams) + StateGraph (durable interrupt/resume on the already-wired SqliteCheckpointStore)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: research.py already runs PlannerOrchestrator + reflect via _build_runtime. Upgrade to a MultiAgentOrchestrator AgentTeam: a 'searcher' (ask_papers + web), an 'analyst' synthesizer, and a 'critic'. Model long reports as a StateGraph with NodeInterrupt so partials persist across restarts. The web leg is keyless DuckDuckGo and usually empty without HIMMY_SEARCH_API_KEY — pair with a search-key setting. Persist briefs (currently saved nowhere) so past research is browsable.

otherFeatures.title: Time-travel + related-facts knowledge graph over memory — MISSED by the proposal
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A 'what did I believe as of <date>' view and a related-items panel surfacing linked notes/papers/tasks for whatever the user is viewing — turning memory from a flat fact list into a navigable graph.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy memory bi-temporal recall (as_of/active_only) + typed MemoryLink graph + traverse_graph + recall(max_hops=)
otherFeatures.howItWorks: The memory-auto-injection feature adopts only the adapter + consolidation; this adds the temporal/graph layer. Use as_of point-in-time recall for the time-travel view and MemoryLink/traverse_graph for a related-facts panel (paper->note->task). A distinctive research-desk feature; build after memory auto-injection.

otherFeatures.title: In-library full-text/semantic search surface (distinct from the chat agent)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A 'search everything I've read' surface over the durable knowledge base — direct semantic/full-text search of the library, not just chat. Today list() filters only title/author/venue/tags; full-text is ONLY via the RAG agent.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy KnowledgeBase.search over the now-durable SqliteKnowledgeBackend
otherFeatures.howItWorks: Once the RAG index is persisted, expose a GET /library/search endpoint backed by KnowledgeBase.search and a search box in the Library tab — nearly free given the durable backend. Depends on the RAG-persistence feature.

otherFeatures.title: Activity & Provenance timeline (EntityRecord/RunEvent audit spine)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: A trustworthy 'what Himmy did' timeline: tools called, what it read, what it remembered, why it chose an answer, with per-answer source links.
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy EntityRecord audit spine + RunEvent stream (TOOL_*, OUTCOME_SCORED, MEMORY_*, LEARNING_APPLIED) read off the storage.db the app already has
otherFeatures.howItWorks: storage.db already exists in .scholar-desk and SqliteSessionStore is wired. Pass an EntityRegistry into MemoryService so memory writes project onto the spine, read the RunEvent stream to build GET /activity, render as a read-only Planner sub-tab (the sub-tab array at App.tsx:1415). EntityLinks relate a derived note to its source paper and the run that produced it. Mostly observability over data already being generated.

otherFeatures.title: Regression benchmark suite for the research agent (internal eng gate)
otherFeatures.whatUserGets: Confidence that any prompt/model/tool change actually improves the assistant before it ships, plus a principled way to pick the smallest local model that still answers well (matters for an offline-first desktop app).
otherFeatures.frameworkCapabilityUsed: himmy BenchmarkRunner + graders (arg-level/trajectory) + judge + stats (`himmy bench`) or AgentEvalHarness.evaluate_agent
otherFeatures.howItWorks: Author a small in-repo YAML suite (ask_papers retrieval, citation formatting, calendar/gmail tool selection, 'should NOT call a tool' irrelevance cases) and run BenchmarkRunner against the app's configured model. Use as a check before changing agent.yaml/prompts/tools and to compare candidate local models. Not user-facing — high developer-velocity value, cheap to stand up.

finalRoadmap.notes: All critique corrections verified against the codebase: (1) requirements.txt is himmy[toolkit,api,openai,embeddings] with NO cron extra; (2) server.py has create_app() at :175 and app=create_app() at :826 with NO lifespan and zero scheduling code; (3) config.py:73-84 has the existing HIMMY_STORE/MEMORY/TASKS/CONVERSATIONS_PATH setdefaults but NO routines pin; (4) papers_rag.py:177/183-184 ALREADY uses build_embedder_and_dim() with backend=None (so the proposal's embedder-swap is wrong — only backend persistence is needed); (5) agent.yaml:142-144 declares guardrails: [pii, injection] and :156 self_learning: true (so the privacy pipeline is already live — only the DLP vault + redaction badge remain); (6) library.py:558-568 backup() zips only library.db + library_files/ and restore is destructive (the missed whole-workspace-backup risk). Two things I could not fully verify without deeper inspection and flagged as VERIFY-FIRST in the roadmap: that resolve_canonical_storage() + studio_service.stream_agent_run run end-to-end through agent_path inside this custom non-BFF app (the spike in sprint item 1 de-risks the foundation's effort estimate), and that electron's Notification import works in main.cjs (confirmed unused, not confirmed importable). The worker (always-on) feature was re-labeled as an EITHER/OR alternative to the in-process scheduler, not an additive dependsOn layer, per the critique — running both means two schedulers (flock-guarded but redundant).
